# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
**"Define substance abuse and addiction. Differentiate between physical dependence and psychological dependence."**

**Model Answer (3-mark scheme):**

| Component | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Definition of substance abuse | 1 | A maladaptive pattern of substance use leading to significant impairment or distress, as per DSM-5/ICD-10 criteria. Continued use despite harm. |
| Definition of addiction | 1 | A chronic, relapsing neurobiological disorder characterized by compulsive drug-seeking, continued use despite harm, and long-lasting brain changes (ASAM/NIDA). |
| Physical vs Psychological dependence | 1 | **Physical dependence** = body adapts; withdrawal symptoms appear on cessation (e.g., tremors, seizures). **Psychological dependence** = emotional/cognitive craving; user believes they cannot cope without the substance (e.g., cravings, anxiety). |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
**"List the clinical features of opioid overdose and its emergency management."**

**Model Answer (3-mark scheme):**

| Component | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Clinical features (triad) | 1.5 | **(1) Respiratory depression** (↓ RR, Cheyne-Stokes, apnea), **(2) Pinpoint pupils (miosis)**, **(3) CNS depression/coma**. Other features: hypotension, bradycardia, hypothermia. |
| Emergency management | 1.5 | **Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM** (pure μ-opioid antagonist; reverses respiratory depression within 1–2 min). Supportive: airway, oxygen, ventilation. Repeat naloxone as its half-life is shorter than most opioids. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE full question** from the choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Describe the **neurobiological basis of addiction** with a special focus on the **mesolimbic dopamine reward pathway**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss in detail the **pharmacology, acute effects, chronic effects, withdrawal features, and treatment of alcohol use disorder**. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for (a) — Neurobiological Basis of Addiction (7 marks)

| Step | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Introduction | 1 | Addiction is a chronic relapsing brain disease. The **mesolimbic dopamine system** (also called the **reward circuit**) is central to its development. |
| 2. Anatomical pathway | 1.5 | **VTA → Nucleus Accumbens → Prefrontal Cortex**. All addictive substances converge here. |
| 3. Dopamine surge mechanism | 1.5 | Drugs cause **5–10× normal dopamine release** in the NAc. Natural rewards (food, sex) cause only modest, regulated release. Drugs bypass natural regulation. |
| 4. Long-term brain changes | 1.5 | Chronic use → **D2 receptor downregulation**, reduced baseline dopamine, **neuroadaptations in PFC** (impaired decision-making), and **strengthening of conditioned cues** (environmental triggers). |
| 5. Clinical correlation | 1.5 | This explains (i) tolerance, (ii) anhedonia during withdrawal, (iii) cravings triggered by cues, (iv) compulsive use despite harm. |

#### Model Answer for (b) — Alcohol Use Disorder (7 marks)

| Section | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Pharmacology** | 1.5 | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) acts as a **CNS depressant** by enhancing **GABA-A** receptor activity (inhibitory) and inhibiting **NMDA glutamate** receptors (excitatory). Also ↑ dopamine in NAc. Metabolized in liver by **alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH)** and **ALDH2** to acetaldehyde, then to acetate. |
| **Acute effects** | 1.5 | Disinhibition, slurred speech, ataxia, impaired judgment, nystagmus, emotional lability, vasodilation. At BAC > 0.30%: coma; > 0.40%: respiratory depression, death. |
| **Chronic effects** | 1.5 | Liver: fatty liver → alcoholic hepatitis → cirrhosis. Brain: **Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome** (thiamine deficiency). CVS: cardiomyopathy, hypertension. Pancreas: chronic pancreatitis. Fetal: **Fetal Alcohol Syndrome**. ↑ Cancer risk (oral, esophageal, hepatic, breast). |
| **Withdrawal stages** | 1.5 | 6–12 h: tremors, anxiety. 12–24 h: hallucinosis. 24–48 h: seizures. 48–96 h: **Delirium Tremens (DTs)** — confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, **mortality 5–15%**. |
| **Treatment** | 1 | **Acute withdrawal**: Diazepam/Chlordiazepoxide (long-acting BZD), **Thiamine** before glucose. **Long-term**: Disulfiram (aversive agent), Naltrexone (μ-antagonist), Acamprosate (NMDA modulator), CBT, AA (Alcoholics Anonymous). |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Classify **psychoactive substances** and describe the **mechanism, acute intoxication features, withdrawal, and management of opioid use disorder**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare and contrast the **effects of cannabis, hallucinogens (LSD), and volatile solvents** on the central nervous system, including their characteristic clinical features and health risks. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for (a) — Opioids (7 marks)

| Section | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | 1 | Based on pharmacology: (1) **Depressants** (alcohol, BZD, barbiturates), (2) **Opioids** (heroin, morphine, fentanyl), (3) **Cannabinoids** (cannabis), (4) **Stimulants** (cocaine, amphetamines, caffeine), (5) **Hallucinogens** (LSD, psilocybin), (6) **Volatile solvents** (toluene), (7) **Tobacco** (nicotine). |
| **Mechanism of opioids** | 1.5 | Bind to **μ (mu), κ (kappa), δ (delta) opioid receptors**. μ-receptor → analgesia, euphoria, respiratory depression, miosis, constipation, dependence. |
| **Acute intoxication** | 1.5 | **Euphoria ("rush")**, pinpoint pupils (miosis — pathognomonic), respiratory depression, hypotension, bradycardia, constipation, nausea, decreased GI motility. |
| **Withdrawal** | 1.5 | Onset 6–12 h, peaks 36–72 h. Mnemonic: **Yawning, Lacrimation, Rhinorrhea, Sweating, Piloerection, Mydriasis, Muscle aches, Diarrhea, Vomiting.** Uncomfortable but rarely fatal (unlike BZD withdrawal). |
| **Management** | 1.5 | **Acute overdose**: Naloxone (μ-antagonist). **Long-term**: Methadone (long-acting μ-agonist substitution), Buprenorphine (partial agonist), Naltrexone (relapse prevention). Behavioral therapy, harm reduction (needle exchange). |

#### Model Answer for (b) — Cannabis vs Hallucinogens vs Volatile Solvents (7 marks)

| Feature | Cannabis | Hallucinogens (LSD) | Volatile Solvents |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | CB1/CB2 receptor agonist (THC mimics anandamide) | 5-HT2A receptor agonist (serotonin mimicry) | Membrane fluidization; NMDA antagonism; GABA potentiation |
| **Acute effects** | Euphoria, red eyes, dry mouth, ↑appetite, slow reflexes, paranoia | Visual hallucinations, synesthesia, time distortion, ego dissolution, dilated pupils | Euphoria, dizziness, slurred speech, hallucinations, "laughing gas" effect |
| **Chronic effects** | Amotivational syndrome, ↓IQ in adolescents, ↑schizophrenia risk, bronchitis | HPPD (flashbacks), persistent psychosis (rare) | Neurotoxicity (cerebellar, peripheral neuropathy), hepatotoxicity, leukemia (benzene), **Sudden Sniffing Death Syndrome (SSDS)** |
| **Withdrawal** | Mild: irritability, sleep disturbance, ↓appetite (1–3 weeks) | Minimal physical dependence; psychological craving possible | Headache, nausea, hallucinations |
| **Special concerns** | Trigger for schizophrenia in genetically predisposed | "Bad trips" with self-harm risk | Used by adolescents (cheap, legal); SSDS can kill within minutes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse the **opioid toxidrome (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression)** with the **sympathomimetic toxidrome (dilated pupils, hypertension, tachycardia)**. Many students mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Do not** state that opioid withdrawal is "life-threatening" — it is **uncomfortable but rarely fatal alone**. **BZD and alcohol withdrawal ARE life-threatening**.
> - **Do not** forget that **DTs has 5–15% mortality** if untreated — examiners specifically test this.
> - **Do not** miss the **pathognomonic sign of opioid intoxication** = pinpoint pupils; examiners award 1 mark for this exact phrase.
> - **Do not** confuse **CAGE questionnaire** (alcohol screening) with **AUDIT** (WHO 10-item alcohol screening) — CAGE is the rapid 4-item tool.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### A. Top 20 Must-Know Facts (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Substance abuse** = maladaptive pattern; **Addiction** = chronic relapsing brain disease with compulsion.
- [ ] Physical dependence ≠ Addiction (insulin example).
- [ ] The **mesolimbic dopamine pathway** (VTA → NAc → PFC) is the **final common pathway** for all addictive drugs.
- [ ] Drugs cause **5–10× normal dopamine release** in NAc.
- [ ] **D2 receptor downregulation** explains tolerance and anhedonia.
- [ ] **Adolescent brain** is most vulnerable (PFC mature only at age 25).
- [ ] **Heritability** of addiction ≈ **40–60%**.
- [ ] **BAC > 0.40%** = potentially fatal (respiratory depression).
- [ ] **Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome** = thiamine deficiency in alcoholics; **Wernicke's triad**: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia.
- [ ] **Delirium Tremens (DTs)** = 48–96 h post-last drink; mortality **5–15%**; emergency.
- [ ] **Opioid overdose triad**: respiratory depression + pinpoint pupils + coma. **Antidote: Naloxone**.
- [ ] **Opioid withdrawal**: "Yawning, Lacrimation, Rhinorrhea, Sweating, Piloerection, Mydriasis, Muscle aches, Diarrhea, Vomiting" — uncomfortable but rarely fatal.
- [ ] **Methadone, Buprenorphine, Naltrexone** = opioid substitution / antagonist therapy.
- [ ] **Cannabis active compound = THC**; binds **CB1 receptors**; chronic use **triggers schizophrenia** in genetically predisposed.
- [ ] **BZD/barbiturate withdrawal IS life-threatening** (seizures, status epilepticus) — unlike opioid withdrawal.
- [ ] **Flumazenil** = BZD antagonist (use cautiously — can precipitate seizures).
- [ ] **Cocaine mechanism**: blocks DA/NE/serotonin reuptake → sympathomimetic toxidrome.
- [ ] **Cocaine crash**: depression, fatigue, cravings (no major medical emergency).
- [ ] **"Cocaine bugs" = formication** = tactile hallucination of insects crawling.
- [ ] **Caffeine = adenosine receptor antagonist**; withdrawal = **headache** (most common).
- [ ] **LSD = 5-HT2A agonist**; **no significant physical dependence**; **HPPD** (flashbacks) can occur.
- [ ] **Tobacco/nicotine**: agonist at nAChR; addiction via dopamine in NAc; treatment: NRT, Bupropion, Varenicline.
- [ ] **Volatile solvent abuse** → **Sudden Sniffing Death Syndrome (SSDS)**; chronic toluene use → cerebellar damage, peripheral neuropathy, leukemia (benzene).
- [ ] **Sympathomimetic toxidrome** (cocaine/amphetamine): dilated pupils, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation. Treatment: **Benzodiazepines**.
- [ ] **Indian legal framework**: **NDPS Act 1985** (narcotic drugs), **COTPA 2003** (tobacco), **Excise Act** (alcohol).

### B. KTU Frequently Asked 5-Word Answer Prompts

1. **"Define substance abuse"** → Maladaptive pattern, harm, continued use.
2. **"Define addiction"** → Chronic, relapsing, compulsive, brain disease.
3. **"Mechanism of cocaine"** → DA/NE/5-HT reuptake inhibition.
4. **"Mechanism of cannabis"** → CB1/CB2 receptor agonist (THC).
5. **"Mechanism of alcohol"** → ↑GABA, ↓Glutamate (NMDA).
6. **"Opioid overdose treatment"** → Naloxone (μ-antagonist).
7. **"Alcohol withdrawal emergency"** → Delirium Tremens (DTs).
8. **"Wernicke-Korsakoff cause"** → Thiamine (B1) deficiency.
9. **"Caffeine mechanism"** → Adenosine receptor antagonist.
10. **"Hallucinogen mechanism"** → 5-HT2A receptor agonist.

### C. Mnemonics to Remember

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **VTA → NAc → PFC** | Reward pathway (Ventral Tegmental Area, Nucleus Accumbens, Prefrontal Cortex) |
| **"Lacrimation, Rhinorrhea, Sweating, Piloerection, Mydriasis, Muscle aches, Diarrhea, Vomiting"** | Opioid withdrawal features |
| **"Pinpoint pupils + Slow breathing + Sleep"** | Opioid overdose triad |
| **"Thiamine Before Glucose"** | In alcoholics, to prevent Wernicke's |
| **"4 D's of alcohol withdrawal"** | DT's, Death risk, Delirium, Diazepam (treatment) |
| **"CAGE"** | Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener (alcohol screening) |

### D. Cross-Cutting Themes for Essay-Type Questions

1. **Prevention strategies**: School-based education, family interventions, restriction of availability, taxation, advertising bans.
2. **Indian context**: NDPS Act 1985, COTPA 2003, role of NIMHANS (Bangalore), AIIMS de-addiction centres.
3. **Public health approach**: Harm reduction vs. abstinence debate (e.g., needle exchange, methadone maintenance).
4. **Relapse as a feature of chronic disease**: Not a moral failure — comparable to relapse in hypertension or diabetes.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL EXAM STRATEGY FOR KTU STUDENTS:**
> 1. **Always** mention **DSM-5 / ICD-10** classification in definitions.
> 2. **Always** link substance effects to the **specific receptor** or **neurotransmitter** involved (mechanism-first thinking).
> 3. **Always** distinguish between **acute intoxication, chronic use, withdrawal, and overdose** — KTU loves this 4-part framework.
> 4. **Always** quote **mortality figures** (e.g., DTs 5–15%) when discussing withdrawal.
> 5. **Use mnemonics** liberally in revision but write **full-form** in the exam.
> 6. **Indian context matters**: NDPS Act, COTPA, Fetal Alcohol Syndrome, socially relevant drugs (bhang, gutka, beedis).

*— End of Module 3 Comprehensive Notes —*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHWT127 - HEALTH AND WELLNESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Primary Survey: ABC (Airway, Breathing, Circulation), Qualities of a Good First Aider, First Aid Measures for Cuts and Scrapes, Bruises, Sprains, Strains, Fractures, Burns, Nosebleeds

# Primary Survey & First Aid Fundamentals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Primary Survey (ABC) — Formal Definition

The **Primary Survey** is the initial, rapid, and systematic assessment conducted by a first aider or rescuer to identify and treat immediate life-threatening conditions in a casualty. The universally accepted acronym is **A-B-C**, which stands for **Airway, Breathing, and Circulation**. It is the foundational algorithm of emergency medical response under protocols such as **DR-ABCDE** (Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) endorsed by the **American Heart Association (AHA)** and the **Red Cross**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The primary survey must be completed within **10–15 seconds** for a conscious casualty and **less than 60 seconds** for an unconscious casualty. The rescuer must NEVER move further down the alphabet until the previous step is stabilized.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The River of Life":**
> Imagine the human body as a vast city. Oxygen is the electricity, blood is the fuel trucks, and the airway is the main highway. In an emergency, you must check the highway first (Airway → can trucks enter?). Then check the power grid (Breathing → is electricity flowing?). Finally, verify the fuel supply (Circulation → is fluid pumping?). If any one of these fails, the city collapses — even if the buildings (bones, muscles) are perfectly intact.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Primary Survey

The following measurable standards are used during ABC assessment:

- **Respiratory Rate (RR):** 12–20 breaths/minute (adult), 15–30 (child), 25–50 (infant).
- **Pulse Rate (PR):** 60–100 beats/minute (adult resting).
- **Capillary Refill Time (CRT):** Less than **2 seconds** is normal.
- **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP):** Normal is **120 mmHg**; below **90 mmHg** indicates shock.
- **Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂):** Above **94%** is acceptable in a healthy adult.

### 1.4 First Aid — Formal Definition

**First Aid** is the immediate, temporary, and non-invasive care given to a person suffering from a minor injury or sudden illness before the arrival of qualified medical help. The legal framework is anchored in the **Good Samaritan Law (India, 2016)** which protects bystanders who assist in good faith.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> *"First Aid is the bridge between injury and treatment — not a substitute for medical care."*

---

## 2. Qualities of a Good First Aider

### 2.1 Definition

A **First Aider** is a trained individual who provides initial emergency care. The **Indian Red Cross Society** and **St. John Ambulance** define the role by both **technical competence** and **personality traits**.

### 2.2 Key Qualities (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Quality | Functional Importance |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Calm & Composed** | Prevents panic transmission to casualty and bystanders. |
| 2 | **Observant** | Detects subtle signs (skin color, breathing pattern, pupil size). |
| 3 | **Resourceful** | Improvises using available items (e.g., shirt as a sling). |
| 4 | **Skilled & Knowledgeable** | Applies correct DR-ABCDE protocol. |
| 5 | **Tactful & Sympathetic** | Provides emotional reassurance. |
| 6 | **Authoritative & Firm** | Takes command at the scene to direct bystanders. |
| 7 | **Self-Disciplined** | Avoids harming casualty through incorrect technique. |
| 8 | **Hygienic** | Uses gloves, sanitizers — prevents infection transmission. |
| 9 | **Physically Fit** | Capable of performing CPR for extended durations. |
| 10 | **Honest & Ethical** | Reports observations accurately to EMS. |

### 2.3 Quick Reference — DO's and DON'Ts

> [!WARNING]
> **DO NOT** move a casualty with suspected spinal injury unless there is an immediate danger (fire, drowning).
> **DO NOT** give food or water to an unconscious casualty.
> **DO NOT** apply a tourniquet unless trained — it can cause limb loss.
> **ALWAYS** call emergency services (**112** in India) early.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 3. The ABC of Primary Survey — Stepwise Breakdown

### 3.1 A — Airway

**Purpose:** Confirm the airway is open and unobstructed.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Assess responsiveness** — Tap shoulders firmly, shout *"Are you okay?"*
2. **Position the casualty** — Place in supine position on a firm, flat surface.
3. **Open the airway** using the **Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift** maneuver (in non-trauma cases).
4. For suspected spinal injury, use the **Jaw-Thrust** maneuver instead.
5. **Look, Listen, and Feel (LLF)** for **10 seconds** to detect breathing.
6. **Sweep the mouth** (finger sweep) only if a solid object is visible — **NEVER** blind sweep.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tongue as the most common obstruction:** In an unconscious casualty lying supine, the tongue falls backward due to loss of muscle tone, blocking the pharynx. The head-tilt-chin-lift maneuver mechanically lifts the tongue away from the posterior pharyngeal wall.

### 3.2 B — Breathing

**Purpose:** Verify effective gas exchange.

**Logic steps:**
1. **Look** for chest rise and fall (symmetry, depth).
2. **Listen** for breath sounds at the mouth/nose.
3. **Feel** for exhaled air on your cheek.
4. **Count respiratory rate** for **30 seconds and multiply by 2**.
5. **Check for abnormalities:** paradoxical breathing (flail chest), Kussmaul breathing (acidotic), Cheyne-Stokes (brain injury).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If breathing is absent but airway is clear → begin Rescue Breathing** (1 breath every 5–6 seconds for adults; 1 breath every 2–3 seconds for infants/children).

### 3.3 C — Circulation

**Purpose:** Assess cardiac output and perfusion.

**Logic steps:**
1. **Check carotid pulse** (adults, >1 year) — at the groove between trachea and sternocleidomastoid.
2. **Check brachial pulse** (infants <1 year) — medial aspect of upper arm.
3. **Capillary Refill Time (CRT):** Press nail bed for 5 seconds; release; color should return in **<2 seconds**.
4. **Check for major hemorrhage** — perform rapid blood sweep (scalp, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, limbs).
5. **Control bleeding** using **Direct Pressure → Elevation → Pressure Points → Tourniquet (last resort)**.

### 3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Vital Sign Reference Table

| Vital Sign | Adult | Child (1–8 yrs) | Infant (<1 yr) | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------------------|----------------|------|
| Respiratory Rate (RR) | 12–20 | 15–30 | 25–50 | breaths/min |
| Heart Rate (HR) | 60–100 | 80–130 | 100–160 | beats/min |
| Systolic BP (SBP) | 90–120 | 80–110 | 70–100 | mmHg |
| Diastolic BP (DBP) | 60–80 | 50–75 | 40–65 | mmHg |
| SpO₂ | ≥ 94 | ≥ 94 | ≥ 92 | % |
| CRT | < 2 | < 2 | < 2 | seconds |
| Temperature | 36.1–37.2 | 36.1–37.8 | 36.6–37.7 | °C |
| Blood Volume | ~ 5 L | ~ 2.5 L | ~ 0.8 L | liters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Nines (Burn Assessment):** Used to estimate % Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) burned. Adult: Head=9%, Each Arm=9%, Anterior Trunk=18%, Posterior Trunk=18%, Each Leg=18%, Genitalia=1%. Pediatric proportions differ due to larger head-to-body ratio.

### 3.5 Formula — Cardiac Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO} &= \text{HR} \times \text{SV} \\
\text{CO}_{\text{normal adult}} &= 70 \text{ bpm} \times 70 \text{ mL} \\
&= 4{,}900 \text{ mL/min} \approx 5 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **CO** = Cardiac Output, **HR** = Heart Rate, **SV** = Stroke Volume. This is clinically relevant because chest compressions in CPR aim to restore partial cardiac output to maintain coronary and cerebral perfusion.

## 4. First Aid for Specific Conditions — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 4.1 Cuts and Scrapes (Abrasions & Lacerations)

**Pathophysiology:** A cut (incision/laceration) is a clean break in skin continuity, while a scrape (abrasion) involves superficial epidermal friction damage.

**First Aid Protocol:**
1. Wash hands thoroughly or wear disposable gloves.
2. Rinse wound under **cool running water** for **5–10 minutes**.
3. Clean around the wound with mild soap (avoid putting soap inside the wound).
4. Apply gentle pressure with a sterile gauze pad to stop bleeding.
5. Apply antibiotic ointment (if available and no allergy).
6. Cover with sterile non-stick dressing and secure with bandage.
7. Seek medical help if: wound is > 1 cm deep, edges gaping, dirt embedded, caused by rusty object, or signs of infection (redness, swelling, pus, fever).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never apply hydrogen peroxide or undiluted alcohol directly on an open wound — it damages tissue and delays healing.

### 4.2 Bruises (Contusions)

**Pathophysiology:** A bruise is caused by **rupture of subcutaneous capillaries** due to blunt trauma, resulting in extravasated blood pooling in tissue.

**Color Progression (KTU High-Yield):**
- **0–2 days:** Reddish-blue (oxyhemoglobin)
- **3–5 days:** Blue-purple (deoxyhemoglobin)
- **5–7 days:** Green (biliverdin)
- **7–14 days:** Yellow-brown (bilirubin and hemosiderin)
- **14–21 days:** Resolves completely

**First Aid — R.I.C.E. Protocol:**
$$
\text{R} \rightarrow \text{Rest}, \quad \text{I} \rightarrow \text{Ice}, \quad \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Compression}, \quad \text{E} \rightarrow \text{Elevation}
$$

- **Rest:** Avoid using injured area.
- **Ice:** Apply cold pack for **15–20 minutes every 1–2 hours** for the first 48 hours.
- **Compression:** Elastic bandage (not too tight).
- **Elevation:** Raise above heart level to reduce swelling.

### 4.3 Sprains vs Strains

**Differentiation (KTU Exam Favorite):**

| Feature | Sprain | Strain |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Tissue affected | Ligament (joint) | Muscle/Tendon |
| Common site | Ankle, wrist, knee | Hamstring, lower back, calf |
| Mechanism | Twisting/joint forced beyond range | Overstretching of muscle |
| Severity grades | I, II, III | I, II, III |
| Pain on | Joint movement | Muscle contraction |

**Severity Grading:**
- **Grade I:** Mild stretching, microscopic tears. Minimal swelling.
- **Grade II:** Partial tear. Moderate pain, swelling, bruising.
- **Grade III:** Complete tear/rupture. Severe pain, instability, possible "pop" sound.

**First Aid:** Same R.I.C.E. protocol. Immobilize joint with elastic bandage. Refer Grade III to orthopedic specialist immediately.

### 4.4 Fractures

**Definition:** A fracture is a complete or partial break in the continuity of bone.

**Types:**
- **Closed (Simple):** Skin intact.
- **Open (Compound):** Bone penetrates skin → high infection risk.
- **Comminuted:** Bone broken into multiple fragments.
- **Greenstick:** Incomplete fracture (children).
- **Spiral, Transverse, Oblique:** Based on fracture line orientation.
- **Pathological:** Due to underlying disease (osteoporosis, cancer).

**Signs & Symptoms (S-L-O-P-D-T):**
- **S**welling
- **L**oss of function
- **O**bvious deformity
- **P**ain (point tenderness)
- **D**iscoloration (bruising)
- **T**enderness over bone
- **C**repitus (grating sound) — do NOT deliberately test.

**First Aid:**
1. **Do NOT** move casualty unless necessary.
2. **Immobilize** the injured area in the position found (splinting).
3. Apply **R.I.C.E.** for closed fractures.
4. Cover open fractures with sterile dressing; do NOT push bone back.
5. Treat for shock — keep warm, reassure.
6. Call emergency services for suspected spine, pelvis, femur, or open fractures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splinting Principles (KTU):**
> - Immobilize joints **above AND below** the fracture site.
> - Pad rigid splints before application.
> - Check distal circulation (pulse, color, sensation) before and after splinting.
> - For suspected spinal fracture, do NOT move casualty — wait for paramedics.

### 4.5 Burns

**Classification:**

| Burn Type | Depth | Appearance | Healing |
|-----------|-------|------------|---------|
| **First-Degree (Superficial)** | Epidermis only | Red, dry, painful (sunburn) | 5–10 days, no scarring |
| **Second-Degree (Partial Thickness)** | Epidermis + Dermis | Blisters, wet, very painful | 2–3 weeks, may scar |
| **Third-Degree (Full Thickness)** | All layers + nerve endings | White/charred, leathery, painless | Requires grafting |
| **Fourth-Degree** | Muscle, tendon, bone | Black, charred | Often fatal |

**Rule of Nines — Burn TBSA Calculation:**
$$
\text{Total TBSA \%} = \sum (\text{Percentage per body region affected})
$$

**First Aid — 4 C's Rule:**
1. **Cool** — Run under cool (NOT ice) water for **10–20 minutes**.
2. **Clear** — Remove jewelry/loose clothing (before swelling).
3. **Cover** — Loose, sterile, non-adhesive dressing or cling film.
4. **Carry/Call** — Shift to hospital if severe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Never apply** ice, butter, toothpaste, or oil on burns. Do NOT break blisters. Avoid immersing large burn areas in cold water (risk of hypothermia).

**Electrical Burns:** Ensure power source is disconnected before approaching. Look for entry and exit wounds. Internal damage may be far worse than external appearance.

**Chemical Burns:** Flush with copious running water for **at least 20 minutes**. Note the chemical substance (bring container to hospital).

### 4.6 Nosebleeds (Epistaxis)

**Definition:** Bleeding from the nasal cavity, most commonly from **Kiesselbach's plexus** (Little's area) on the anterior nasal septum — responsible for ~**90%** of cases.

**Types:**
- **Anterior Epistaxis:** 90% of cases, easier to control.
- **Posterior Epistaxis:** 10%, more severe, requires medical intervention.

**First Aid Protocol:**
1. Reassure casualty — anxiety elevates blood pressure, worsening bleeding.
2. Sit casualty upright, leaning **slightly forward** (NOT backward — prevents blood ingestion and aspiration).
3. Pinch the soft part of the nose (just below the bony bridge) for **10–15 minutes** continuously.
4. Apply cold compress over the bridge of the nose to cause vasoconstriction.
5. Breathe through the mouth; do NOT blow, sniff, or tilt head back.
6. After 15 minutes, release pressure gently. If bleeding continues, repeat for another 15 minutes.
7. Seek medical help if: bleeding persists > 20 minutes, follows head injury, blood volume > 200 mL, or casualty is on blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism of Pinching:** Direct mechanical compression of the nasal alae against the septum occludes Kiesselbach's plexus vessels, allowing platelet aggregation and clot formation.

### 4.7 Real-World Engineering/Workplace Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Relevance — Why First Aid Knowledge Matters for Engineers:**
> - **Industrial Safety Compliance:** Factories, construction sites, and labs require certified first aiders under the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 45)** and **OSHA** guidelines.
> - **Disaster Response:** Engineers deployed in disaster zones (earthquakes, floods) must perform triage.
> - **Project Management:** Safety officers coordinate on-site first aid posts.
> - **Ethical Responsibility:** NEP 2020 emphasizes holistic wellness; first aid is a core life-skill.

# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Protocols

## 5. Complete Step-by-Step Primary Survey Algorithm (KTU Analytical Matrix)

### 5.1 Full Derivation of the Primary Survey Sequence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision Path:} \quad & \text{DANGER} \rightarrow \text{RESPONSE} \rightarrow \text{AIRWAY} \\
& \rightarrow \text{BREATHING} \rightarrow \text{CIRCULATION} \\
& \rightarrow \text{DISABILITY} \rightarrow \text{EXPOSURE}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 5.2 Stage 1 — D (Danger)

**Step-by-step:**
1. Survey the scene for hazards — fire, traffic, electrical wires, gas leaks, unstable structures, aggressive bystanders.
2. If hazard exists, eliminate it OR relocate the casualty (only if safe).
3. **Decision logic:** Is the scene safe? If NO → move casualty; If YES → proceed to Response.

### 5.3 Stage 2 — R (Response)

**Step-by-step:**
1. Tap both shoulders firmly.
2. Shout loudly near the ear: *"Can you hear me? Open your eyes!"*
3. **Decision logic:**
   - Responds verbally → monitor and treat visibly injured areas.
   - Responds to pain only → casualty is semi-conscious; proceed to A.
   - No response → casualty is unconscious; immediately call EMS (112), proceed to A.

### 5.4 Stage 3 — A (Airway) — Exhaustive Logical Steps

**Step-by-step with decision branches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A.1:} & \quad \text{Clear visible obstruction from mouth.} \\
\text{Step A.2:} & \quad \text{Choose maneuver based on suspected trauma:} \\
& \quad \text{(a) No trauma} \rightarrow \text{Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift} \\
& \quad \text{(b) Trauma suspected} \rightarrow \text{Jaw-Thrust only} \\
\text{Step A.3:} & \quad \text{Open airway and Listen-Feel-Look for 10 seconds.} \\
\text{Decision:} & \quad \text{Airway clear?} \rightarrow \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{proceed to B} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{reposition and recheck.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift Technique:**
- Place one hand on forehead.
- Apply firm backward pressure with palm.
- Place fingertips of other hand under the chin.
- Lift chin upward and forward — this lifts the tongue from the posterior pharynx.

**Jaw-Thrust Technique (Trauma):**
- Place fingers behind the angle of the mandible (jaw bone).
- Displace mandible forward and upward WITHOUT tilting the head.

### 5.5 Stage 4 — B (Breathing) — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step-by-step:**
1. Place ear 2–3 cm above casualty's mouth, facing chest.
2. **Look** for chest rise/fall (mirror fogging in cold weather can assist).
3. **Listen** for breath sounds (gurgling, wheezing, stridor).
4. **Feel** for air on cheek.
5. Count rate over 30 seconds × 2.
6. Check for symmetrical chest expansion.

**Decision Branches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition:} & \quad \text{Apneic (no breathing)} \rightarrow \text{Deliver 2 rescue breaths} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{Reassess} \\
\text{Condition:} & \quad \text{Gasping/agonal breathing} \rightarrow \text{Treat as apneic} \\
\text{Condition:} & \quad \text{Normal breathing} \rightarrow \text{Place in recovery position}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recovery Position (3/4 Prone, Lateral):** Used for unconscious, breathing casualties. The casualty is placed on their side to prevent aspiration of vomit. Key steps: extend near arm at right angle; flex far knee; roll casualty toward you; tilt head back slightly; ensure airway remains open.

### 5.6 Stage 5 — C (Circulation) — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step-by-step:**
1. Palpate **carotid pulse** for **no more than 10 seconds**.
2. **Simultaneous hemorrhage control scan** — run gloved hands under casualty, check for wetness (blood).
3. Apply direct pressure to any bleeding site using sterile gauze and elevation.

**Decision Branches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulse present + breathing:} & \rightarrow \text{Continue monitoring; recovery position.} \\
\text{Pulse absent + breathing absent:} & \rightarrow \text{Initiate CPR (30:2 ratio).} \\
\text{Pulse absent + breathing present:} & \rightarrow \text{Rare — continue CPR; treat for shock.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 5.7 CPR Compression Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compression Rate (adults)} &= 100 \text{ to } 120 \text{ compressions/minute} \\
\text{Compression Depth (adults)} &= \text{at least } 5 \text{ cm, not exceeding } 6 \text{ cm} \\
\text{Compression-to-Ventilation Ratio} &= 30:2 \text{ (single rescuer)} \\
\text{Cycle Length} &= 30 \text{ compressions} + 2 \text{ breaths} \approx 18 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hand Placement:** Lower half of the sternum (center of chest), heel of one hand, other hand on top, fingers interlocked, arms straight, shoulders directly above hands.

### 5.8 Shock — Pathophysiology & First Aid

**Shock** is a life-threatening condition of inadequate tissue perfusion.

**Types:**
- **Hypovolemic:** Blood/fluid loss.
- **Cardiogenic:** Pump failure.
- **Distributive:** Sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic.
- **Obstructive:** Tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade.

**Signs & Symptoms (KTU Mnemonic — "SHOCKED"):**
- **S**kin pale, cold, clammy
- **H**eart rate rapid (>100 bpm)
- **O**utput (urine) decreased
- **C**onfusion, anxiety
- **K**een (thirst)
- **E**xertion (dyspnea)
- **D**ecreased BP (late sign)

**First Aid:**
1. Lay casualty flat, elevate legs (unless spinal injury suspected).
2. Loosen tight clothing.
3. Cover with blanket to maintain body temperature.
4. Do NOT give food or water.
5. Reassure casualty.
6. Monitor ABC continuously.
7. Call EMS immediately.

## 6. Detailed Clinical Calculation Examples

### 6.1 Burn TBSA Calculation Using Rule of Nines

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Example:} \quad & \text{Adult with burns on entire right arm and anterior trunk.} \\
\text{Right Arm:} \quad & = 9\% \\
\text{Anterior Trunk:} \quad & = 18\% \\
\text{Total TBSA burned:} \quad & = 9\% + 18\% = 27\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 6.2 Pediatric Modification (Lund-Browder Chart)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pediatric Head (at birth)} & = 18\% \rightarrow 9\% \text{ (adult)} \\
\text{Pediatric Each Leg (at birth)} & = 14\% \rightarrow 18\% \text{ (adult)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale:** A newborn's head represents a larger proportion of body surface area compared to an adult due to the cephalocaudal development gradient.

### 6.3 Blood Volume Loss Estimation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class I Hemorrhage:} \quad & < 15\% \text{ blood volume} \rightarrow \text{minimal symptoms} \\
\text{Class II Hemorrhage:} \quad & 15\text{–}30\% \rightarrow \text{anxiety, tachycardia} \\
\text{Class III Hemorrhage:} \quad & 30\text{–}40\% \rightarrow \text{confusion, BP drop} \\
\text{Class IV Hemorrhage:} \quad & > 40\% \rightarrow \text{lethal without intervention}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Adult blood volume} &= 5 \text{ L} \\
\text{Class III threshold} &= 0.40 \times 5 \text{ L} = 2.0 \text{ L loss} \\
\text{Symptom threshold} &= 0.30 \times 5 \text{ L} = 1.5 \text{ L loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 7. Mermaid Diagrams — Visual Architecture

### 7.1 Primary Survey Flowchart (ABC Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Casualty Found]) --> B{DANGER Present?}
    B -- YES --> C[Remove Hazard OR Relocate Casualty]
    B -- NO --> D[RESPONSE Check: Tap and Shout]
    C --> D
    D -- No Response --> E[Call EMS: 112]
    D -- Responds --> F[Monitor and Treat Visible Injuries]
    E --> G[A: AIRWAY]
    G --> G1[Open Airway: Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift or Jaw-Thrust]
    G1 --> G2{Airway Clear?}
    G2 -- NO --> G3[Reposition and Recheck]
    G3 --> G2
    G2 -- YES --> H[B: BREATHING]
    H --> H1[Look Listen Feel for 10 seconds]
    H1 --> H2{Breathing?}
    H2 -- NO --> H3[2 Rescue Breaths]
    H3 --> I
    H2 -- YES --> I[C: CIRCULATION]
    I --> I1[Check Pulse and Hemorrhage]
    I1 --> I2{Pulse Present?}
    I2 -- NO --> J[Begin CPR 30:2 Ratio]
    I2 -- YES --> K[Recovery Position]
    J --> I
    K --> L[Continuous Monitoring]
    L --> M([EMS Arrives])
```

### 7.2 First Aid Decision Tree for Common Injuries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Injury Type Identified]) --> B{Cuts or Scrapes?}
    A --> C{Bruise or Swelling?}
    A --> D{Sprain or Strain?}
    A --> E{Fracture Suspected?}
    A --> F{Burn?}
    A --> G{Nosebleed?}

    B -- YES --> B1[Clean with Water 5-10 min]
    B1 --> B2[Apply Direct Pressure]
    B2 --> B3[Sterile Dressing]
    B3 --> B4{Deep or Long?}
    B4 -- YES --> B5[Seek Medical Help]
    B4 -- NO --> B6[Monitor for Infection]

    C -- YES --> C1[Apply Cold Pack 15-20 min]
    C1 --> C2[Elevate Area]
    C2 --> C3[Monitor Color Progression]

    D -- YES --> D1[RICE Protocol]
    D1 --> D2{Severe Pain or Instability?}
    D2 -- YES --> D3[Immobilize and Refer]
    D2 -- NO --> D4[Continue RICE 48 hours]

    E -- YES --> E1[Do NOT Move Casualty]
    E1 --> E2[Immobilize Joints Above and Below]
    E2 --> E3[Cover Open Wound if Present]
    E3 --> E4[Call EMS Immediately]

    F -- YES --> F1[Degree Classification]
    F1 --> F2[Cool Water 10-20 min]
    F2 --> F3[Cover with Sterile Dressing]
    F3 --> F4{Large TBSA or Deep?}
    F4 -- YES --> F5[Call EMS for Shock]
    F4 -- NO --> F6[Monitor and Refer]

    G -- YES --> G1[Sit Upright Lean Forward]
    G1 --> G2[Pinch Soft Nose 10-15 min]
    G2 --> G3{Bleeding Stops?}
    G3 -- NO --> G4[Repeat 15 min]
    G4 --> G5{Persists over 20 min?}
    G5 -- YES --> G6[Seek Medical Help]
```

### 7.3 Burn Depth Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Burn Assessment]) --> B{Depth of Tissue Damage?}
    B -- Epidermis Only --> C[First Degree: Superficial]
    B -- Epidermis Plus Dermis --> D[Second Degree: Partial Thickness]
    B -- Full Skin Layers --> E[Third Degree: Full Thickness]
    B -- Muscle Tendon Bone --> F[Fourth Degree: Life Threatening]

    C --> C1[Symptoms: Redness, Pain, No Blisters]
    C1 --> C2[Healing: 5 to 10 Days]

    D --> D1[Symptoms: Blisters, Severe Pain, Wet Appearance]
    D1 --> D2[Healing: 2 to 3 Weeks]

    E --> E1[Symptoms: White Leathery Painless]
    E1 --> E2[Healing: Requires Skin Graft]

    F --> F1[Symptoms: Black Charring]
    F1 --> F2[Healing: Often Fatal]

    C2 --> G[First Aid Protocol]
    D2 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> H[Emergency Medical Care]
    G --> I[Cool Water 10 to 20 minutes]
    I --> J[Sterile Non-Adhesive Cover]
    J --> K[Monitor for Shock]
```

### 7.4 R.I.C.E. Protocol Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REST: Avoid Movement] --> B[ICE: Cold Pack 15 to 20 min]
    B --> C[COMPRESSION: Elastic Bandage]
    C --> D[ELEVATION: Above Heart Level]
    D --> E((Injury Stabilized))

    A1[Repeat Every 1 to 2 hours] -.-> B
    B1[First 48 Hours Only] -.-> C
    C1[Not Too Tight] -.-> D
    D1[Reduces Swelling] -.-> E
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 8. Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Primary Survey. Explain the significance of the ABC protocol in emergency first aid.**

**Model Answer:**
The Primary Survey is a rapid, systematic assessment performed by a first aider to identify and manage immediate life-threatening conditions. ABC stands for **Airway, Breathing, and Circulation**. It ensures oxygen delivery to vital organs and prevents death from airway obstruction, respiratory failure, or cardiac arrest. The protocol must be completed within 10–60 seconds and forms the foundation of emergency medical response worldwide.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any six qualities of a good First Aider and briefly explain their importance.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Calm** – Prevents panic transmission to casualty and bystanders.
2. **Observant** – Detects subtle signs like skin color changes.
3. **Resourceful** – Improvises with available materials.
4. **Hygienic** – Uses gloves to prevent cross-infection.
5. **Sympathetic** – Provides emotional reassurance to casualty.
6. **Self-Disciplined** – Avoids harmful interventions; follows protocol strictly.

---

## 9. Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — Module 4, 14-Mark Choice Question

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the Primary Survey with the A-B-C protocol. Explain each step with its clinical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the first aid management of burns. Classify burns and explain the Rule of Nines with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Primary Survey — Detailed Description:**

The Primary Survey is a quick, structured assessment using the **DR-ABCDE** algorithm. Its purpose is to identify and immediately manage life-threatening conditions.

**A — Airway (2 Marks):**
- Tap and shout for response.
- Use **Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift** for non-trauma; **Jaw-Thrust** for trauma.
- Look, Listen, Feel for breathing for 10 seconds.
- Clear foreign bodies with finger sweep (only if visible).

**B — Breathing (2 Marks):**
- Check chest rise/fall, breath sounds, air movement.
- Count respiratory rate.
- If absent, deliver 2 rescue breaths; if present, place in recovery position.

**C — Circulation (2 Marks):**
- Palpate carotid pulse (adult) or brachial pulse (infant) for ≤10 seconds.
- Check **Capillary Refill Time (<2 seconds)**.
- Control hemorrhage using direct pressure, elevation, pressure points, or tourniquet (last resort).
- Treat for shock.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** ABC must be sequential — never skip a step. Each stage addresses the most immediate threat to life.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Burns — First Aid and Classification:**

**Classification (3 Marks):**

| Type | Depth | Appearance |
|------|-------|------------|
| First-Degree | Epidermis | Red, painful, no blisters |
| Second-Degree | Epidermis + Dermis | Blisters, severe pain |
| Third-Degree | Full skin | White/leathery, painless |
| Fourth-Degree | Muscle/Bone | Charred, black |

**Rule of Nines (3 Marks):**

Adult Rule of Nines: Head 9%, each arm 9%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, each leg 18%, genitalia 1%.

**Numerical Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case:} \quad & \text{Burns on entire left leg and anterior trunk} \\
\text{Left Leg:} \quad & = 18\% \\
\text{Anterior Trunk:} \quad & = 18\% \\
\text{Total TBSA:} \quad & = 18\% + 18\% = 36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**First Aid — 4 C's (1 Mark):**
1. **Cool** water 10–20 minutes.
2. **Clear** jewelry.
3. **Cover** with sterile dressing.
4. **Call** EMS for severe burns.

---

## 10. Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` — Alternative Question

**Q: (a)** Explain the first aid measures for fractures. Differentiate between sprains and strains. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the emergency management of nosebleeds (epistaxis). Explain its mechanism and when medical help is required. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Fractures — First Aid (4 Marks):**
1. Do NOT move casualty unless in immediate danger.
2. Immobilize the fracture site — splint with rigid support, padding.
3. Pad splints to prevent pressure points.
4. Immobilize joints **above AND below** the fracture.
5. Check distal circulation (pulse, color, sensation) before/after.
6. Cover open fractures with sterile dressing (no bone reinsertion).
7. Treat for shock; keep warm; reassure.
8. Call EMS for spine, pelvis, femur, or compound fractures.

**Sprains vs Strains — Differentiation Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Sprain | Strain |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Tissue | Ligament | Muscle/Tendon |
| Site | Joints (ankle, wrist) | Muscles (hamstring, calf) |
| Severity | Grade I, II, III | Grade I, II, III |
| R.I.C.E. indicated | Yes | Yes |

**Conclusion:** Both injuries use R.I.C.E. management but referral criteria differ — Grade III of either requires specialist care.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Nosebleeds (Epistaxis) — Emergency Management (5 Marks):**

**Mechanism:** 90% of nosebleeds originate from **Kiesselbach's plexus** (anterior nasal septum), a vascular network susceptible to trauma and dryness.

**First Aid Protocol:**
1. Sit casualty upright, lean **slightly forward** (prevents blood aspiration).
2. Pinch soft part of the nose (below bony bridge) for **10–15 minutes**.
3. Apply cold compress over nasal bridge → vasoconstriction.
4. Breathe through mouth; do NOT blow nose.
5. Reassess after 15 minutes; repeat if needed.
6. After bleeding stops, avoid nose blowing for several hours.

**When to Seek Medical Help (2 Marks):**
- Bleeding persists beyond **20 minutes**.
- Bleeding is posterior (blood flowing down throat).
- Volume exceeds **200 mL**.
- History of head injury or hypertension.
- Casualty on **anticoagulants** (aspirin, warfarin).
- Recurrent nosebleeds in children (may indicate underlying condition).

---

## 11. KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Leading to Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Order Confusion in ABC:** Writing BCA or CAB — ALWAYS ABC. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Incorrect Head Position in Nosebleed:** Writing "tilt head BACK" — WRONG. Must be forward.
> 3. **Applying Ice Directly on Burn:** Always use COOL running water; never apply ice or butter.
> 4. **Moving a Spinal Injury Casualty:** This is the most common critical error. Only move if there is *immediate danger* (fire).
> 5. **Confusing Sprain and Strain:** Sprain = ligament; Strain = muscle/tendon. Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Forgetting to Call EMS:** Many answers treat the casualty but forget to mention dialing **112**.
> 7. **Rule of Nines Calculation Errors:** Children have a different head-to-leg ratio; do not use adult values for pediatric cases.

---

## 12. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4:**

- **ABC = Airway → Breathing → Circulation** (never skip a step).
- **Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift** for non-trauma; **Jaw-Thrust** for trauma.
- **Recovery Position:** Unconscious + breathing casualty.
- **CPR Ratio:** 30 compressions : 2 breaths; depth 5–6 cm; rate 100–120/min.
- **Pulse Check Site:** Carotid (adult/child); Brachial (infant).
- **CRT Normal:** Less than 2 seconds.
- **Sprain = Ligament** | **Strain = Muscle/Tendon**.
- **R.I.C.E.** for bruises, sprains, strains: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation.
- **Fracture Splinting Rule:** Immobilize joints above AND below.
- **Burns — 4 C's:** Cool, Clear, Cover, Call.
- **Rule of Nines Adult:** 9-9-18-18-18-9-1.
- **Burns — DO NOT apply** ice, butter, toothpaste, or break blisters.
- **Nosebleed Position:** Lean **FORWARD**, pinch **soft part** for 10–15 minutes.
- **Epistaxis Source:** Kiesselbach's plexus (90% of cases).
- **Emergency Number India:** **112**.
- **Good Samaritan Law (2016):** Protects bystanders assisting in good faith.
- **Primary Survey Time:** 10–15 seconds (conscious), < 60 seconds (unconscious).
- **Respiratory Rate:** 12–20 (adult), 15–30 (child), 25–50 (infant).
- **Severe Burn Referral:** > 10% TBSA in adults, > 5% in children.
- **Shock Position:** Flat with legs elevated (unless spinal injury).
- **First Aider Qualities:** Calm, observant, resourceful, hygienic, sympathetic, authoritative.
- **Factories Act 1948 (Sec 45):** Mandates trained first aiders in industrial workplaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Sprain vs Strain:** **"S-L-M-T"** → **S**prain = **L**igament | **S**train = **M**uscle/**T**endon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Primary Survey Sequence:** **"A-Bee-C"** → **A**irway first, **B**reathing next, **C**irculation last — just like unlocking a chain link by link.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Aid Procedures: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR), Heimlich Maneuver, Applying a Sling, Sports Injuries: Classification (Soft Tissue Injuries - Abrasion, Contusion, Laceration, Incision, Sprain and Strain)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCHWT127 - HEALTH AND WELLNESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** First Aid Procedures: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR), Heimlich Maneuver, Applying a Sling, Sports Injuries: Classification (Soft Tissue Injuries - Abrasion, Contusion, Laceration, Incision, Sprain and Strain)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 First Aid — The Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **First Aid** is the immediate, temporary, and often life-saving care given to a person who has been injured or has fallen suddenly ill, before the arrival of qualified medical assistance. It is built on three pillars: **Preserve Life**, **Prevent the Condition from Worsening**, and **Promote Recovery**.

The word **"First"** in First Aid is non-negotiable — it is not a substitute for medical treatment; it is the *bridge* between the incident and the hospital. The **"Golden Hour"** concept in emergency medicine states that a victim's chances of survival are highest if definitive care is received within **60 minutes** of the incident.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of First Aid as the *emergency patch* you apply to a leaking pipe before the plumber arrives. You are not fixing the pipe, but you are stopping the water (blood/breath) from gushing out. If you do nothing, the room floods (organ failure/death). If you call the plumber (ambulance) but do not turn off the valve (First Aid), the plumber arrives to find a destroyed room.

---

## 1.2 Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) — Definition

> [!NOTE] **Core Definition**
> **Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)** is an emergency lifesaving procedure performed when the heart stops beating (cardiac arrest) or breathing ceases. It combines **chest compressions** (mimicking the heart's pumping action) with **rescue breaths** (mimicking lung ventilation) to maintain circulatory flow and oxygenation to vital organs — especially the brain.

**Key Metric to Remember:** The brain suffers irreversible damage within **4 to 6 minutes** of oxygen deprivation. CPR buys time by manually circulating oxygenated blood.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a water pump (the heart) that has stopped. You cannot fix the pump right now, but you can keep water (blood) flowing through the pipes (arteries) by manually squeezing the main pipe (chest compressions) and blowing air (rescue breaths) into the system until the electrician (paramedic with defibrillator) arrives.

---

## 1.3 Heimlich Maneuver — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Heimlich Maneuver** (abdominal thrusts) is an emergency First Aid technique used to treat upper airway obstruction caused by a **foreign body** (commonly food) lodged in the trachea or throat. It works by creating an **artificial cough** through sudden, forceful inward and upward pressure on the diaphragm, expelling the obstruction.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Picture a cork stuck in a wine bottle. Shaking the bottle does not work. But if you strike the bottom firmly, the pressure inside rises sharply and pops the cork out. The Heimlich Maneuver is that firm strike — it forces the diaphragm up, compressing the lungs, and the air rushes out with enough velocity to dislodge the stuck object.

---

## 1.4 Applying a Sling — Definition

> [!NOTE] **Core Definition**
> A **Sling** is a triangular bandage (or improvised cloth) used as a First Aid device to **immobilize and support** an injured upper limb (arm, shoulder, collarbone, or hand). It reduces pain, prevents further injury, and keeps the limb in an elevated, stable position to minimize swelling.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a sling as a *hammock* for the injured arm. Just as you would not let a broken chair leg bear weight, you should not let an injured arm hang freely or be moved. The sling cradles the limb, sharing the gravitational load with the neck and opposite shoulder.

---

## 1.5 Sports Injuries — Soft Tissue Injury Classification

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> **Soft Tissue Injuries** are damages to muscles, ligaments, tendons, skin, and connective tissues — as opposed to fractures (bone injuries). The **five classical types** classified in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: **Abrasion, Contusion, Laceration, Incision, and Sprain/Strain**.

| S.No | Injury Type | Structure Damaged | Everyday Analogy |
|------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Abrasion | Superficial skin (epidermis) | Scuffing your knee on the road |
| 2 | Contusion | Blood vessels under skin (bruise) | Getting hit by a cricket ball |
| 3 | Laceration | Skin and deeper tissue (tear/irregular edges) | Skin tearing on a rough metal edge |
| 4 | Incision | Skin and deeper tissue (clean cut) | Paper cut or surgical scalpel cut |
| 5 | Sprain | Ligaments (joint) | Twisting your ankle |
| 6 | Strain | Muscle or tendon | Pulling a hamstring while running |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal First Aid Framework — "DRSABCD"

> [!NOTE] The KTU 2024 syllabus endorses the internationally accepted **DRSABCD Action Plan** (used by St. John Ambulance, Red Cross, and WHO-affiliated first aid bodies) for all emergency response.

The acronym **DRSABCD** stands for:

- **D — Danger:** Check the scene for hazards (traffic, fire, electric wires) before approaching. *You cannot help if you become a victim.*
- **R — Response:** Check the victim's responsiveness. Tap shoulders firmly, shout *"Are you okay?"* 
- **S — Send for Help:** Call emergency services (India: **112**; Kerala: **108** ambulance) immediately.
- **A — Airway:** Tilt the head back, lift the chin to open the airway. Check for obstructions.
- **B — Breathing:** Look, Listen, and Feel for breathing for **no more than 10 seconds**.
- **C — CPR:** If not breathing normally, begin chest compressions and rescue breaths.
- **D — Defibrillation:** Apply an Automated External Defibrillator (AED) if available.

---

## 2.2 CPR — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Physiology of CPR

CPR works on the principle of **artificial circulation**. When the heart stops, the residual oxygen in the blood (about **4–6 minutes' worth**) can be circulated to the brain and heart if external pressure is applied to the chest. Each compression squeezes the heart between the **sternum (breastbone)** and the **spine**, ejecting blood into the systemic circulation.

### 2.2.2 Compression Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Compression Metrics (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **Compression Depth:** At least **5 cm (2 inches)** but not more than **6 cm (2.4 inches)** in adults.
> - **Compression Rate:** **100 to 120 compressions per minute** (the tempo of the song *"Stayin' Alive"* by Bee Gees).
> - **Compression-to-Ventilation Ratio:** **30:2** (30 compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths).
> - **Hand Placement:** Lower half of the **sternum** (center of the chest), heel of one hand, other hand on top, fingers interlocked, arms straight, shoulders directly over the hands.
> - **Recoil:** Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions — do not lean on the chest.

### 2.2.3 Rescue Breathing

- Pinch the nose shut, seal your mouth over the victim's mouth, and give **2 breaths**, each lasting **1 second**, watching for chest rise.
- If chest does not rise, re-check the airway (head tilt-chin lift).
- If unwilling to give breaths, perform **Hands-Only CPR** (continuous chest compressions).

---

## 2.3 Heimlich Maneuver — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 The Physics of Abdominal Thrusts

When a foreign body blocks the trachea, the lungs still contain residual air. By applying sudden inward and upward pressure just **above the navel** (at the **xiphoid process** level), the diaphragm is forced upward into the thoracic cavity. This compresses the lungs and creates a high-velocity upward airflow through the trachea — generating an **artificial cough** of approximately **30–40 liters per minute** of expiratory flow, which is sufficient to expel the obstruction.

### 2.3.2 Universal Sign of Choking

> [!WARNING] **The Universal Choking Sign:** A person who is choking typically clutches their throat with one or both hands. If the person can cough forcefully, encourage them to continue. If they **cannot cough, speak, or breathe**, the Heimlich Maneuver is required immediately.

---

## 2.4 Applying a Sling — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.4.1 Types of Slings

| Sling Type | Purpose | Used For |
|------------|---------|----------|
| **Arm Sling** | Supports the forearm in a horizontal position | Lower arm, wrist, hand injuries |
| **Elevated Sling** | Holds the hand higher than the elbow | Hand injuries, to reduce bleeding/swelling |
| **Collar-and-Cuff Sling** | Supports the wrist; allows the elbow to hang | Upper arm/shoulder injuries, fractured collarbone |

### 2.4.2 The Triangular Bandage

The standard sling uses a **triangular bandage** (approximately **90 cm × 90 cm × 130 cm**). It is folded into a broad-fold or used as-is for an arm sling.

---

## 2.5 Soft Tissue Injuries — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 The R.I.C.E. Protocol (Initial Management)

For most soft tissue injuries (sprain, strain, contusion), the immediate First Aid is the **R.I.C.E.** protocol:

- **R — Rest:** Stop the activity; avoid movement that causes pain.
- **I — Ice:** Apply ice (wrapped in cloth) for **15–20 minutes** every 1–2 hours for the first **48 hours**.
- **C — Compression:** Apply an elastic bandage snugly (not too tight) to reduce swelling.
- **E — Elevation:** Raise the injured limb **above heart level** to reduce swelling via gravity-assisted venous return.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Modern Update — The P.R.I.C.E. and P.E.A.C.E. & L.O.V.E. Frameworks**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus recommends knowing that newer guidelines (e.g., British Journal of Sports Medicine) have evolved to include **Protection** (P.R.I.C.E.) and, more recently, the **P.E.A.C.E. & L.O.V.E.** acronym, which emphasizes avoiding anti-inflammatory medications in the acute phase to allow natural healing. However, the traditional **R.I.C.E.** remains the most widely taught in Indian first-aid curricula.

### 2.5.2 Sprain vs. Strain — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield KTU Question Trigger**
> - **Sprain:** Injury to a **ligament** (the tissue connecting bone to bone at a joint). Caused by twisting or wrenching. Common sites: ankle, wrist, knee.
> - **Strain:** Injury to a **muscle or tendon** (the tissue connecting muscle to bone). Caused by overstretching or overuse. Common sites: hamstring, lower back, calf.

**Memory Trick:** *"Ligaments = Joints = Sprains"* and *"Muscles/Tendons = Movement = Strains."*

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Summary Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | CPR | Heimlich Maneuver | Sling | R.I.C.E. |
|-----------|-----|-------------------|-------|----------|
| **When to Use** | Cardiac arrest / no breathing | Choking (cannot cough/speak) | Arm/shoulder/hand injury | Sprain, strain, contusion |
| **Key Action** | Chest compressions 30:2 | Abdominal thrusts (inward & upward) | Triangular bandage immobilization | Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation |
| **Critical Number** | 100–120 compressions/min; 5–6 cm depth | 5 thrusts until object expelled or person becomes unconscious | Arm at ~90° to body, hand slightly elevated | Ice 15–20 min every 1–2 hr |
| **Emergency Number (India)** | 112 / 108 | 112 / 108 | Routine care | Routine care |
| **Key Caution** | Do not lean on chest between compressions | Do NOT use on infants < 1 year (use back blows) | Do not wrap too tightly — check finger circulation | Do NOT apply ice directly to skin |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Note:** Procedure-based questions (especially CPR and Heimlich Maneuver) carry high weightage. The evaluator looks for **sequential accuracy**, **mentioning key numbers** (compression depth, rate, ratio), and **safety cautions**. Memorize the steps in the exact order given below.

---

## 3.1 CPR — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure (Adults)

### Phase 1: Assessment (DRSABCD)

**Step 1 — Ensure Scene Safety (Danger)**
Scan the environment for hazards. Move the victim only if there is immediate danger. Do not become a casualty yourself.

**Step 2 — Check Responsiveness (Response)**
Tap the victim's shoulders firmly. Shout near the ear, *"Are you okay? Can you hear me?"* If there is no response, treat as unconscious.

**Step 3 — Call for Help (Send)**
Shout for bystanders. Direct a specific person: *"You — call 112/108 and bring an AED."* Bystanders obey specific commands more reliably than general shouts.

**Step 4 — Open the Airway (Airway)**
Place one hand on the forehead, two fingers of the other hand under the chin. Tilt the head back gently and lift the chin. This is the **Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift** maneuver.

**Step 5 — Check Breathing (Breathing)**
With the airway open, place your ear near the victim's mouth. **Look** for chest movement, **Listen** for breath sounds, and **Feel** for air on your cheek. Do this for **no more than 10 seconds**. Gasping is NOT normal breathing.

### Phase 2: Action (CPR)

**Step 6 — Position Yourself**
Kneel beside the victim at chest level. Place the heel of one hand on the **lower half of the sternum** (center of the chest, between the nipples). Place the other hand on top, interlock fingers, keep arms straight.

**Step 7 — Begin Compressions**
Push down hard and fast. Compress **at least 5 cm (2 inches)** but no more than **6 cm (2.4 inches)**. Allow the chest to fully recoil after each compression. Do not lean on the chest.

**Step 8 — Count the Compressions**
Count aloud: *"1 and 2 and 3 and ... up to 30."* Maintain a rate of **100–120 per minute**. After every **30 compressions**, give **2 rescue breaths**.

**Step 9 — Deliver Rescue Breaths**
Pinch the nose. Take a normal breath. Seal your mouth over the victim's mouth and blow steadily for **1 second**, watching for chest rise. Give the second breath the same way. Then resume 30 compressions.

**Step 10 — Continue the Cycle**
Repeat the **30:2** cycle until:
- The victim shows signs of life (breathing, movement).
- An AED is available.
- Trained medical help takes over.
- You are too exhausted to continue.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake:** Applying the wrong depth (too shallow) or wrong rate (too slow). The KTU 2024 examiner specifically checks for the **5–6 cm depth** and **100–120/min rate**.

---

## 3.2 Heimlich Maneuver — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure

### 3.2.1 For a Conscious Adult / Child (> 1 year)

**Step 1 — Identify Choking**
Ask: *"Are you choking?"* If the person cannot speak, cough, or breathe, and shows the **universal choking sign** (hands clutching throat), act immediately.

**Step 2 — Position Yourself**
Stand behind the victim. Wrap both arms around their waist. Lean the person slightly forward.

**Step 3 — Locate the Hand Position**
Make a fist with one hand. Place the **thumb side** of the fist against the victim's **abdomen**, in the midline, **slightly above the navel** and **well below the breastbone (xiphoid process)**.

**Step 4 — Grasp and Thrust**
Grasp the fist with the other hand. Deliver a **quick, forceful inward and upward thrust** — as if trying to lift the person. Each thrust is a separate, distinct movement.

**Step 5 — Repeat**
Perform thrusts until the foreign object is expelled (often the person will cough, breathe, or speak) OR until the victim becomes unconscious.

### 3.2.2 If the Victim Becomes Unconscious

- Carefully lower the victim to the ground.
- Call emergency services (112/108) immediately.
- Begin **CPR** (starting with 30 chest compressions).
- **Before giving rescue breaths**, open the mouth and look for the foreign object. If visible, remove it with a finger sweep. **Do NOT perform a blind finger sweep** — it may push the object deeper.

### 3.2.3 Special Case — Infants (< 1 year)

> [!WARNING] **DO NOT use abdominal thrusts on infants.** Use **5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts** instead.

- Place the infant face-down on your forearm (supporting the head and jaw).
- Deliver **5 firm back blows** between the shoulder blades using the heel of your hand.
- Turn the infant face-up on your forearm (head lower than trunk).
- Deliver **5 chest thrusts** using two fingers on the lower sternum.
- Alternate back blows and chest thrusts until the object is expelled or the infant becomes unconscious.

---

## 3.3 Applying a Sling — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure (Arm Sling)

**Step 1 — Reassure the Victim**
Inform them about what you are doing. Ask them to support the injured arm with their other hand if possible.

**Step 2 — Position the Triangular Bandage**
Place the triangular bandage **open** with the **longest edge** (the base) along the victim's uninjured side. The **apex (point)** of the triangle should be on the **injured side**, beyond the elbow.

**Step 3 — Support the Arm**
Gently place the injured forearm across the victim's chest, with the fingers pointing toward the opposite shoulder. The wrist and hand should be slightly **higher than the elbow** (this reduces swelling).

**Step 4 — Bring the Lower End Up**
Bring the **lower end of the bandage up over the forearm** so that it meets the apex point at the shoulder on the injured side.

**Step 5 — Tie the Knot**
Tie the two ends together at the side of the neck (on the uninjured side) using a **reef knot**. The knot should be placed on the side of the neck — **NOT pressing on the spine**.

**Step 6 — Secure the Apex**
Twist the apex of the bandage at the elbow and tuck it in, or pin it with a safety pin to keep the elbow secure.

**Step 7 — Check Circulation**
Press the victim's fingernail — it should turn pale and return to pink within **2 seconds** when released. If it stays white or the fingers become blue/numb, the sling is too tight; loosen it immediately.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Soft Tissue Injury Classification

| S.No | Injury | Tissue Affected | Wound Edge | Bleeding | Cause | First Aid |
|------|--------|-----------------|------------|----------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Abrasion** | Epidermis (superficial skin) | None — scraped | Minor oozing/serous fluid | Friction/scraping (e.g., fall on gravel) | Clean with antiseptic, cover with sterile dressing |
| 2 | **Contusion** | Subcutaneous blood vessels | Intact skin | Internal (bruise) | Blunt force impact (e.g., ball hit) | R.I.C.E. protocol; cold compress |
| 3 | **Laceration** | Skin + deeper tissue | **Irregular, jagged, torn** | Moderate to heavy | Sharp uneven surface, crushing | Control bleeding with direct pressure; suturing may be needed |
| 4 | **Incision** | Skin + deeper tissue | **Clean, straight, sharp** | Heavy (often profuse) | Sharp blade, glass, knife | Direct pressure; suturing required |
| 5 | **Sprain** | Ligament (joint) | N/A (internal injury) | Internal/swelling | Twisting/wrenching of joint | R.I.C.E. + immobilization |
| 6 | **Strain** | Muscle / Tendon | N/A (internal injury) | Internal/swelling | Overstretching/overuse | R.I.C.E. + rest |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Distinction Question — Laceration vs. Incision**
> This is one of the most frequently asked KTU questions. The key difference is the **nature of the wound edges**: A **laceration has torn, irregular edges** (caused by blunt or crushing force), while an **incision has clean, sharp, straight edges** (caused by a sharp object). Bleeding is usually heavier in incisions because cleanly cut blood vessels bleed freely, while crushed vessels in lacerations may seal off partially.

### 3.4.1 Grading of Sprains and Strains

**Sprain Grades:**
- **Grade I (Mild):** Stretching of ligament fibers, mild pain, minimal swelling.
- **Grade II (Moderate):** Partial tearing of ligament, moderate pain, swelling, some loss of function.
- **Grade III (Severe):** Complete rupture of ligament, severe pain, marked swelling, joint instability.

**Strain Grades:**
- **Grade I (Mild):** Mild stretching, minor pain, no loss of strength.
- **Grade II (Moderate):** Partial muscle/tendon tear, pain, swelling, reduced strength.
- **Grade III (Severe):** Complete rupture, severe pain, total loss of function, possible palpable gap.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE] The following Mermaid diagrams are designed to be **block-level functional flow representations** suitable for KTU examination answers. They translate physical procedures into logical sequences that can be reproduced on paper.

---

## 4.1 CPR Decision Flow (DRSABCD Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Scene Reached]) --> dangerA{Danger Present?}
    dangerA -- YES --> safeA[Make Area Safe]
    safeA --> dangerA
    dangerA -- NO --> respA[Check Response Tap Shoulders Shout]
    respA --> rescuerA{Responding?}
    rescuerA -- YES --> monA[Monitor and Reassess]
    rescuerA -- NO --> helpA[Call 112 or 108 Send for AED]
    helpA --> airA[Open Airway Head Tilt Chin Lift]
    airA --> breathA[Look Listen Feel for Breathing Max 10 sec]
    breathA --> breathnA{Breathing Normally?}
    breathnA -- YES --> recovA[Place in Recovery Position Monitor]
    breathnA -- NO --> cprA[Begin CPR 30 Compressions 2 Breaths]
    cprA --> cprLoopA{Duration}
    cprLoopA -- Signs of Life --> recovA
    cprLoopA -- AED Available --> defibA[Apply AED Follow Voice Prompts]
    cprLoopA -- Help Arrives --> recovA
    cprLoopA -- Exhausted --> stopA[Stop Only if Unable to Continue]
```

---

## 4.2 Heimlich Maneuver Flow (Conscious Adult)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Suspected Choking]) --> idB[Identify Universal Choking Sign Hands at Throat]
    idB --> coughB{Can the Person Cough Speak or Breathe?}
    coughB -- YES --> encB[Encourage Strong Coughing Monitor Closely]
    coughB -- NO --> posB[Stand Behind Victim Wrap Arms Around Waist Lean Forward]
    posB --> fistB[Make a Fist Place Thumb Side on Abdomen Above Navel Below Breastbone]
    fistB --> graspB[Grasp Fist with Other Hand]
    graspB --> thrustB[Deliver Quick Inward and Upward Thrust]
    thrustB --> loopB{Object Expelled?}
    loopB -- NO --> thrustB
    loopB -- YES --> monB[Monitor Victim Seek Medical Check]
    thrustB -.-> unconsB[If Victim Becomes Unconscious]
    unconsB[If Victim Becomes Unconscious] --> lowerB[Lower to Ground Carefully]
    lowerB --> callB[Call 112 or 108 Immediately]
    callB --> cprB[Begin CPR 30 Compressions 2 Breaths]
    cprprB[Before Breaths Open Mouth Look for Object]
    cprprB --> visB{Object Visible?}
    visB -- YES --> sweepB[Remove with Finger Sweep]
    visB -- NO --> cprprB
    sweepB --> cprprB
```

---

## 4.3 Arm Sling Application — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Injured Arm Identified]) --> calmC[Reassure Victim Ask to Support Arm]
    calmC --> prepC[Take Triangular Bandage Open Form]
    prepC --> posC[Place Base Along Uninjured Side Apex Toward Injured Side]
    posC --> armC[Gently Position Forearm Across Chest Fingers Toward Opposite Shoulder]
    armC --> elevC[Ensure Hand is Slightly Higher Than Elbow]
    elevC --> wrapC[Bring Lower End Up Over Forearm to Meet Apex at Injured Shoulder]
    wrapC --> knotC[Tie Ends at Side of Neck on Uninjured Side with Reef Knot]
    knotC --> apexC[Twist and Tuck Apex at Elbow Secure with Pin]
    apexC --> checkC[Check Fingernail Capillary Refill Within 2 Seconds]
    checkC --> circC{Circulation Normal?}
    circC -- NO --> loosC[Loosen Sling Recheck]
    loosC --> checkC
    circC -- YES --> doneC[Sling Applied Successfully]
```

---

## 4.4 Soft Tissue Injury Classification — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN([Soft Tissue Injuries]) --> sgroup[Closed Injuries Skin Intact]
    rootN --> ogroup[Open Injuries Skin Broken]
    sgroup --> contN[Contusion Bruise from Blunt Impact]
    sgroup --> sprainN[Sprain Ligament Injury at Joint]
    sgroup --> strainN[Strain Muscle or Tendon Injury]
    ogroup --> abrN[Abrasion Superficial Scrape of Epidermis]
    ogroup --> lacN[Laceration Irregular Torn Edges]
    ogroup --> incN[Incision Clean Sharp Straight Edges]
    sprainN --> sgrade1[Grade I Mild Stretching]
    sprainN --> sgrade2[Grade II Partial Tear]
    sprainN --> sgrade3[Grade III Complete Rupture]
    strainN --> tgrade1[Grade I Mild Overstretch]
    strainN --> tgrade2[Grade II Partial Tear]
    strainN --> tgrade3[Grade III Complete Rupture]
```

---

## 4.5 R.I.C.E. Protocol — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startR([Soft Tissue Injury Detected]) --> restR[REST Stop Activity Avoid Painful Movement]
    restR --> iceR[ICE Apply Wrapped Ice Pack 15 to 20 Minutes]
    iceR --> compR[COMPRESSION Apply Elastic Bandage Snugly Not Too Tight]
    compR --> elevR[ELEVATION Raise Limb Above Heart Level]
    elevR --> monR[Monitor for 48 Hours]
    monR --> docR[If No Improvement Seek Medical Help]
```
