# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define First Aid. List the three primary aims of First Aid.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
First Aid is the **immediate assistance or care given to a person who has been injured or has suddenly fallen ill**, before professional medical help arrives. It is temporary in nature and not a replacement for medical treatment.

**Three Primary Aims (KTU Must-Memorize):**
1. **To Preserve Life** — keep the victim alive (e.g., CPR, controlling bleeding).
2. **To Prevent the Condition from Worsening** — stop further harm (e.g., immobilizing fractures, R.I.C.E. for sprains).
3. **To Promote Recovery** — aid healing (e.g., bandaging, positioning, reassurance).

> *(Word limit: 80–100 words. Use bullet points in exam for clarity.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between a Laceration and an Incision.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Laceration | Incision |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Cause** | Blunt or crushing force; tearing | Sharp object (knife, blade, glass) |
| **Wound Edges** | Irregular, jagged, torn | Clean, sharp, straight |
| **Depth** | Variable, often superficial to deep | Usually deep, uniform depth |
| **Bleeding** | Moderate (crushed vessels may seal) | Heavy (cleanly cut vessels bleed freely) |
| **Healing** | Slower; higher infection risk | Faster; cleaner wound edges |
| **Example** | Skin torn on jagged metal | Surgical cut, paper cut |

*(Use a comparison table in exam for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** (OR). The structure is: **Question A OR Question B**, each carrying **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A | 14 Marks]**
> **(a) What is Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)? Describe the step-by-step procedure for performing CPR on an adult victim. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Explain the Heimlich Maneuver. How would you perform it on (i) a conscious adult, and (ii) an infant below 1 year? (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(a) — CPR Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) is a life-saving emergency procedure that combines **chest compressions** (to mimic heart pumping) and **rescue breaths** (to mimic lung ventilation) when a person has suffered cardiac arrest or stopped breathing normally. It maintains oxygenated blood flow to the brain and heart until advanced medical help arrives.

**Key Numbers to Quote (for full marks):**
- Compression rate: **100–120 per minute**
- Compression depth: **at least 5 cm, not more than 6 cm**
- Ratio: **30 compressions : 2 breaths**
- Hand placement: **Lower half of sternum, center of chest**

**Step-by-Step Procedure (6 marks):**

1. **Scene Safety (DRSABCD — Danger):** Check for hazards. Move the victim only if necessary.
2. **Check Response:** Tap shoulders, shout *"Are you okay?"* — if no response, treat as unconscious.
3. **Call for Help:** Shout for bystanders; direct one to call **112/108** and bring an AED.
4. **Open Airway:** Perform Head-Tilt-Chin-Lift maneuver.
5. **Check Breathing:** Look, Listen, Feel for **no more than 10 seconds**. Gasping is NOT normal breathing.
6. **Position Hands:** Heel of one hand on lower sternum, other hand on top, fingers interlocked, arms straight.
7. **Begin Compressions:** Push hard and fast — 5–6 cm depth, 100–120/min rate. Allow full chest recoil. Count to 30.
8. **Give 2 Rescue Breaths:** Pinch nose, seal mouth, blow for 1 second each. Watch for chest rise.
9. **Continue 30:2 Cycles** until signs of life, AED arrival, or medical takeover.
10. **If unwilling to give breaths:** Perform Hands-Only CPR (continuous compressions).

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(b) — Heimlich Maneuver (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Heimlich Maneuver is an emergency First Aid technique used to relieve **upper airway obstruction (choking)** caused by a foreign body. It uses sudden **abdominal thrusts** to create an artificial cough, expelling the obstruction.

**(i) Conscious Adult (3 marks):**
1. Confirm choking — person cannot cough, speak, or breathe; **universal sign** (hands at throat).
2. Stand behind the victim, wrap arms around the waist, lean the person slightly forward.
3. Make a fist; place the **thumb side** against the abdomen, **above the navel and below the breastbone (xiphoid process)**.
4. Grasp the fist with the other hand; deliver **quick, forceful inward and upward thrusts**.
5. Repeat thrusts until the object is expelled OR the victim becomes unconscious.
6. If unconscious → lower to ground → call 112/108 → begin **CPR** (30:2).

**(ii) Infant below 1 year (3 marks):**
> **DO NOT use abdominal thrusts on infants.**

1. Place infant **face-down** on your forearm, supporting head and jaw; head slightly lower than trunk.
2. Deliver **5 firm back blows** between the shoulder blades (heel of hand).
3. Turn infant **face-up** on your other forearm.
4. Deliver **5 chest thrusts** with two fingers on the lower sternum.
5. Alternate **5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts** until object is expelled or infant becomes unconscious.

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B | 14 Marks]**
> **(a) What is a Sling? Describe the step-by-step procedure for applying an arm sling using a triangular bandage. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Classify Soft Tissue Injuries. Explain the R.I.C.E. protocol for the management of sprains and strains. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer for Q.B(a) — Applying a Sling (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **sling** is a First Aid device (usually made from a triangular bandage) used to **immobilize and support an injured upper limb** (arm, shoulder, collarbone, hand). It reduces pain, prevents further injury, and minimizes swelling by keeping the limb elevated and stable.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (6 marks):**

1. **Reassure the Victim** and ask them to support the injured arm with the opposite hand.
2. **Position the Triangular Bandage** — open, with the base along the uninjured side and the apex pointing toward the injured elbow.
3. **Place the Forearm** gently across the victim's chest, with fingers pointing toward the opposite shoulder. Ensure the **hand is slightly higher than the elbow** (reduces swelling).
4. **Bring the Lower End Up** — fold the lower part of the bandage up over the forearm to meet the apex at the injured shoulder.
5. **Tie a Reef Knot** — tie the two ends together at the side of the **neck on the uninjured side**. The knot should **NOT press on the spine**.
6. **Secure the Apex** — twist the apex point at the elbow and tuck it in, or pin it with a safety pin.
7. **Check Circulation** — press the victim's fingernail; it should blanch and return to pink within **2 seconds**. If not, loosen the sling.

**Cautions to mention:** Avoid knots on the spine; do not wrap too tightly; check fingers for color, warmth, and sensation.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.B(b) — Soft Tissue Classification & R.I.C.E. (7 Marks)**

**Classification of Soft Tissue Injuries (4 marks):**
Soft tissue injuries are classified into **closed** (skin intact) and **open** (skin broken):

- **Closed Injuries:**
 - **Contusion:** Bruise from blunt force; subcutaneous blood vessel damage.
 - **Sprain:** Ligament injury at a joint (e.g., ankle twist).
 - **Strain:** Muscle or tendon overstretch (e.g., hamstring pull).

- **Open Injuries:**
 - **Abrasion:** Superficial scrape of the epidermis (e.g., gravel rash).
 - **Laceration:** Torn, irregular wound edges (e.g., jagged metal cut).
 - **Incision:** Clean, sharp, straight wound edges (e.g., knife cut).

**R.I.C.E. Protocol for Sprains and Strains (3 marks):**

- **R — Rest:** Stop the activity; avoid weight-bearing or painful movement.
- **I — Ice:** Apply **ice wrapped in a cloth** (never directly on skin) for **15–20 minutes** every 1–2 hours for the first 48 hours. Reduces swelling and pain.
- **C — Compression:** Apply an **elastic bandage** snugly (not too tight) to minimize swelling.
- **E — Elevation:** Raise the injured limb **above the level of the heart** to reduce swelling through gravity-assisted venous drainage.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost Here**
> 1. **Confusing Sprain and Strain:** Students often swap them. **Sprain = Ligament; Strain = Muscle/Tendon.** A single swap costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping Key Numbers:** In CPR, forgetting to mention **5–6 cm depth** and **100–120/min rate** is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Incorrect Knot Position in Sling:** Tying the sling knot over the **spine** (instead of the side of the neck) is a procedural error — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Using Heimlich on Infants:** Many students write "use abdominal thrusts for infants" — this is a **dangerous error**; use 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts instead. Examiner penalizes **heavily**.
> 5. **Abrasion vs. Laceration Confusion:** An abrasion does NOT have wound edges (skin is scraped, not cut). A laceration has torn, irregular edges. Confusing these costs definition marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Universal Choking Sign:** Forgetting to mention the **clutching of the throat** as the universal sign of choking loses 1 mark in Heimlich questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **First Aid Aims:** **Preserve Life → Prevent Worsening → Promote Recovery.** The **Golden Hour** is **60 minutes**.
- **DRSABCD:** **Danger → Response → Send → Airway → Breathing → CPR → Defibrillation.**
- **CPR Numbers:** **100–120/min rate, 5–6 cm depth, 30:2 ratio.** Hands-Only CPR is acceptable if unwilling to give breaths.
- **Heimlich Position:** **Above navel, below breastbone (xiphoid process)**. **Inward and UPWARD** thrusts. **Infants: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NEVER abdominal thrusts).**
- **Universal Choking Sign:** Hands clutched at the throat.
- **Sling Knot:** **Side of neck, on the uninjured side** (NEVER over the spine). Hand slightly higher than elbow.
- **Sprain vs. Strain:** **Sprain = Ligament (Joint). Strain = Muscle/Tendon (Movement).**
- **Laceration vs. Incision:** **Laceration = Irregular/Torn edges.** **Incision = Clean/Sharp edges.** Incisions bleed more.
- **R.I.C.E.:** **Rest, Ice (15–20 min), Compression (snug not tight), Elevation (above heart).**
- **Emergency Numbers India:** **112** (unified) / **108** (Kerala ambulance).
- **Abrasion** = epidermis scrape; **Contusion** = bruise under intact skin; **Laceration** = torn wound; **Incision** = clean cut; **Sprain** = ligament; **Strain** = muscle/tendon.
- **Caution:** Never apply ice directly to skin; never tie sling too tight (check capillary refill); never use Heimlich on infants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Need and Importance of Physical Education

### Formal Definition
**Physical Education (PE)** is a structured educational discipline focused on developing and maintaining physical fitness, motor skills, knowledge, and social interaction through movement, sport, exercise, and physical activity. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, it is recognized as a multidisciplinary field integrating biology, psychology, sociology, and public health.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Physical education is not merely "exercise" — it is an *educational process* aimed at holistic development (physical, mental, emotional, and social).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the human body as a **car engine**. If a car is parked for months without use, the battery dies, the oil thickens, and the engine seizes. Similarly, the human body without physical activity becomes stiff, weak, and prone to "breakdown" (lifestyle diseases). Physical Education is the **regular servicing, oiling, and test-driving** that keeps the body running smoothly for decades.

### Why It Is Needed
- **Physiological:** Strengthens cardiovascular system, improves lung capacity, builds muscle and bone density.
- **Psychological:** Releases endorphins (natural mood elevators), reduces cortisol (stress hormone), improves self-esteem.
- **Social:** Teaches teamwork, leadership, discipline, and sportsmanship.
- **Cognitive:** Boosts memory, concentration, and academic performance (proven by neuroscience studies).
- **Preventive:** Reduces risk of lifestyle diseases like Type 2 diabetes, hypertension, obesity, and depression.

### Importance in Interdisciplinary Context

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Interdisciplinary Relevance (Highly Exam-Relevant)**

| Discipline | Relevance of Physical Education |
|---|---|
| **Engineering** | Reduces fatigue in long work hours, prevents Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI), improves focus during design/coding tasks. |
| **Medicine** | Forms basis of preventive healthcare, rehabilitation, and exercise prescription. |
| **Management/Humanities** | Enhances leadership, team-building, time management, and stress handling. |
| **Computer Science** | Counteracts sedentary lifestyle, combats digital eye strain and posture issues. |
| **Architecture** | Improves spatial awareness, stamina for site visits. |
| **Biotechnology** | Understanding of body biomechanics, muscle physiology, and nutrition. |

The **WHO recommends 150 minutes** of moderate-intensity or **75 minutes** of vigorous-intensity physical activity per week for adults aged 18–64.

---

## 1.2 Understanding of the Endocrine System

### Formal Definition
The **Endocrine System** is a network of **glands** that produce and secrete **hormones** directly into the bloodstream to regulate distant target organs, maintaining **homeostasis**, growth, metabolism, reproduction, and behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU exams):** Unlike the *nervous system* (fast, electrical, short-lived), the *endocrine system* is **slow, chemical-based, and long-lasting**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine the body as a **large company**. The **nervous system** is the *phone call* — instant, direct, short message. The **endocrine system** is the *official company-wide email* — slower, but reaches every department, has a lasting effect, and follows a proper approval hierarchy (hypothalamus → pituitary → target gland → hormone → effect).

### Major Glands & Their Hormones (KTU High-Yield)

| Gland | Location | Key Hormones | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hypothalamus** | Brain | Releasing hormones | Master controller, links nervous & endocrine systems |
| **Pituitary** | Brain (base) | GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, Prolactin | "Master gland" — controls other glands |
| **Thyroid** | Neck | T3, T4, Calcitonin | Regulates metabolism, growth |
| **Parathyroid** | Neck (behind thyroid) | Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) | Calcium & phosphorus balance |
| **Adrenal** | Top of kidneys | Adrenaline, Cortisol, Aldosterone | Fight-or-flight, stress response, electrolyte balance |
| **Pancreas** | Abdomen | Insulin, Glucagon | Blood sugar regulation |
| **Pineal** | Brain | Melatonin | Sleep-wake cycle |
| **Gonads** (Ovaries/Testes) | Pelvis/Scrotum | Estrogen, Progesterone, Testosterone | Secondary sexual characteristics, reproduction |
| **Thymus** | Chest (upper) | Thymosin | Immune system maturation |

---

## 1.3 Developing a Fitness Profile

### Formal Definition
A **Fitness Profile** is a comprehensive assessment of an individual's current health and physical status, based on measurable parameters such as **BMI, body composition, cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, flexibility, and resting heart rate**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A fitness profile is like a **report card for your body**. Just as a school report card tells you where you stand in each subject, a fitness profile tells you the "grade" of your heart, lungs, muscles, and flexibility. Without it, you are exercising blindly.

### Key Parameters (KTU Mandatory to Know)
- **BMI (Body Mass Index):** $BMI = \dfrac{Weight (kg)}{[Height (m)]^2}$
- **Resting Heart Rate (RHR):** Measured in beats per minute (bpm)
- **VO₂ Max:** Maximum oxygen uptake during exercise (ml/kg/min)
- **Body Fat Percentage:** Essential for assessing obesity
- **Flexibility Tests:** Sit-and-reach test
- **Strength Tests:** Handgrip dynamometer, push-ups
- **Endurance Tests:** 12-minute Cooper run, 1.5-mile run

---

## 1.4 Healthy Food Habits for Prevention and Progression of Lifestyle Diseases

### Formal Definition
**Healthy food habits** refer to a balanced, regular, and mindful pattern of food consumption that provides all essential nutrients in correct proportions, preventing **lifestyle diseases** such as obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and certain cancers.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of food as **fuel for a car**. If you fill a premium car with low-grade fuel, it will run — but slowly, with knock sounds (symptoms), and the engine (body) will wear out faster. Healthy food is the **premium fuel** that keeps the engine humming for years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Model (KTU 2024 Recommended):**
> - ½ plate → **Vegetables & Fruits**
> - ¼ plate → **Whole Grains / Cereals**
> - ¼ plate → **Proteins** (dal, eggs, fish, lean meat)
> - Plus a small portion of **healthy fats** and **dairy**.

### Lifestyle Diseases Caused by Poor Habits
- Obesity
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
- Hypertension (high BP)
- Atherosclerosis / Coronary Artery Disease
- Stroke
- Osteoporosis
- Certain Cancers (colon, breast)

---

## 1.5 Processed Foods and Unhealthy Eating Habits

### Formal Definition
**Processed foods** are food items that have been altered from their natural state through methods like adding salt, sugar, oil, preservatives, or artificial chemicals for shelf-life, convenience, or taste.

> [!NOTE]
> **NOVA Classification (used globally):**
> - **Group 1:** Unprocessed or minimally processed (fresh fruits, milk, eggs)
> - **Group 2:** Processed culinary ingredients (oil, salt, sugar)
> - **Group 3:** Processed foods (cheese, bread, canned vegetables)
> - **Group 4:** **Ultra-processed foods (UPF)** — soft drinks, chips, instant noodles, packaged snacks

### Conceptual Analogy
Processed/ultra-processed food is like a **fast-pass ticket to a sugar-and-salt trap**. They are designed in labs to hit the "bliss point" — the perfect combination of sugar, salt, and fat that makes your brain crave more, even when you are full.

### Unhealthy Eating Habits (KTU Frequently Asked)
- **Skipping breakfast**
- **Eating while watching screens** (mindless eating)
- **Late-night heavy meals**
- **Excess sugar, salt, and saturated fat**
- **Irregular meal timings**
- **Carbonated drinks replacing water**
- **Emotional eating** (eating due to stress, boredom, or sadness)

### Health Consequences
- Insulin resistance → Type 2 diabetes
- High LDL cholesterol → heart disease
- Fatty liver (NAFLD)
- Dental caries
- Hormonal imbalance
- Obesity and joint problems

---

## 1.6 Depression, Anxiety, Stress

### Formal Definitions

**Depression:** A mood disorder characterized by persistent feelings of sadness, loss of interest, low energy, and impaired cognitive function lasting more than 2 weeks. (Medically: Major Depressive Disorder)

**Anxiety:** A state of excessive worry, nervousness, and fear about future events, often with physical symptoms like palpitations, sweating, and trembling.

**Stress:** The body's non-specific response to any demand or threat (real or perceived), triggering the **"Fight, Flight, or Freeze"** response.

### Conceptual Analogy
- **Stress** is the *alarm bell ringing* in your body.
- **Anxiety** is the *alarm bell stuck in "ON" position*, even when there is no fire.
- **Depression** is the *battery drained out* — the alarm won't even ring, because the system has shut down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Connection:** All three involve the **HPA axis** (Hypothalamus–Pituitary–Adrenal) and elevated **cortisol** levels, often with reduced **serotonin** and **dopamine**.

### Common Symptoms
| Disorder | Key Symptoms |
|---|---|
| **Stress** | Irritability, muscle tension, fatigue, insomnia |
| **Anxiety** | Excessive worry, palpitations, sweating, trembling, panic |
| **Depression** | Persistent sadness, anhedonia (no pleasure), hopelessness, sleep/appetite changes, suicidal thoughts |

### Healthy Coping Strategies
- Regular physical exercise
- Adequate sleep (7–9 hours)
- Mindfulness, meditation, deep breathing
- Social connection and support
- Professional counselling (psychologist/psychiatrist)
- Avoiding alcohol, tobacco, and substance abuse
- Time management and balanced lifestyle

---

## 1.7 Different Ways of Carrying an Injured Person

### Formal Definition
**Emergency Carries / Lifts** are techniques used by first responders or bystanders to transport an injured or unconscious person from a dangerous or inaccessible location to a safer place for medical care, when standard ambulance transport is unavailable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (KTU 2024):** *Never move an injured person unless there is immediate danger.* Improper carrying can worsen spinal injuries, fractures, or internal bleeding.

### Conceptual Analogy
Carrying an injured person is like carrying a **fragile glass vase full of water**. You must support it firmly, keep it level, and avoid sudden jerks — otherwise, the vase breaks (bones fracture further) or water spills (internal injury worsens).

### Major Types of Carries
1. **One-rescuer carries:** Cradle carry, Fireman's carry, Piggyback, Drag/Sheet pull
2. **Two-rescuer carries:** Two-handed seat carry, Four-handed seat carry, Human crutch
3. **Multiple-rescuer carries:** Three-person lift, Blanket/Stretcher carry
4. **Improvised carries:** Using chairs, blankets, or poles as stretchers

*(Detailed diagrams in Section 4)*

---

## 1.8 Usage of Automated External Defibrillator (AED)

### Formal Definition
An **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** is a portable, life-saving electronic device that diagnoses **life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias** (ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia) and delivers an **electrical shock** to restore normal heart rhythm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** AEDs are designed for use by **laypersons** (non-medical people) with minimal training. The device itself gives voice prompts.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine the heart is a **drummer who has lost his rhythm** — instead of beating in steady "thump-thump," he is drumming chaotically or has stopped completely. The AED is a **metronome with a loud electric shock** that resets the drummer's rhythm back to normal.

### When to Use
- Sudden **cardiac arrest** (no pulse, no breathing)
- Person collapsed and unresponsive
- Confirmed by trained rescuer

### How It Works (Stepwise)
1. **Power ON** the device
2. **Attach electrode pads** (one on upper right chest, one on lower left side)
3. **Analyze rhythm** — device does this automatically
4. **Stand clear** if shock advised
5. **Press shock button** (semi-automatic AED)
6. **Resume CPR** immediately after shock
7. Continue until medical help arrives

### Key Cautions
- Do not use on a person who is **wet** or lying in **water**
- Do not touch patient during shock delivery
- Remove **medication patches** (e.g., nitroglycerin) before applying pads
- Keep **oxygen sources** at least 1 meter away during shock
- **Pacemakers** (small bump under skin) — place pad at least 8 cm away
- Use **pediatric pads** for children under 8 years

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & HIGH-YIELD POINTS

## 2.1 Physical Education — The 4 Pillars of Fitness

The KTU 2024 scheme identifies four interconnected components of fitness:

1. **Health-Related Fitness**
   - Cardiovascular endurance
   - Muscular strength
   - Muscular endurance
   - Flexibility
   - Body composition

2. **Skill-Related Fitness**
   - Agility, Balance, Coordination
   - Power, Reaction time, Speed

3. **Mental Fitness**
   - Stress management, Emotional regulation, Self-efficacy

4. **Social Fitness**
   - Teamwork, Communication, Leadership, Empathy

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloom's Taxonomy Application (KTU 2024):** At the *Understand* level, students must describe these components. At the *Apply* level, they must design a weekly fitness plan.

---

## 2.2 Endocrine System — Feedback Mechanism

The body uses **negative feedback loops** to maintain balance:

$$\text{Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Gland} \rightarrow \text{Hormone released} \rightarrow \text{Effect} \rightarrow \text{Response reduces stimulus}$$

### Example: Thyroid Regulation
1. Hypothalamus releases **TRH** (Thyrotropin-Releasing Hormone)
2. Pituitary releases **TSH** (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone)
3. Thyroid releases **T3 / T4**
4. T3/T4 increase metabolism
5. **High T3/T4 → inhibits TRH and TSH** (negative feedback)

### Stress Response (HPA Axis)
$$\text{Stress} \rightarrow \text{Hypothalamus (CRH)} \rightarrow \text{Pituitary (ACTH)} \rightarrow \text{Adrenal (Cortisol)} \rightarrow \text{Body's stress response}$$

Chronic stress → **chronically high cortisol** → suppresses immunity, damages memory, increases belly fat.

---

## 2.3 Fitness Profile — Interpretation of BMI

| BMI Range | Category | Health Risk |
|---|---|---|
| < 18.5 | Underweight | Malnutrition risk |
| 18.5 – 24.9 | **Normal** | Low |
| 25.0 – 29.9 | Overweight | Moderate |
| 30.0 – 34.9 | Obese Class I | High |
| 35.0 – 39.9 | Obese Class II | Very High |
| ≥ 40 | Obese Class III (Morbid) | Extremely High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Limitation Note:** BMI does not distinguish between fat and muscle mass. Athletes may have high BMI but low body fat. Hence, **Body Fat %** and **Waist-Hip Ratio** are more accurate.

### Cooper Test (12-minute run)
- **VO₂ Max estimation:** $VO_2 = (Distance\ in\ meters - 504.9) \div 44.73$
- Excellent for men: > 2.4 km; for women: > 2.0 km (approximate)
- **Target Heart Rate:** $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$
- **Training zone (60–80%):** $HR_{target} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) \times (220 - Age)$

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Purpose | Normal Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ | Body mass index | 18.5 – 24.9 |
| $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$ | Maximum heart rate | bpm |
| $HR_{target} = \% \times HR_{max}$ | Training heart rate | 60–80% |
| $Waist\text{-}Hip\ Ratio = \dfrac{Waist}{Hip}$ | Cardiovascular risk | M < 0.9, F < 0.85 |
| $Daily\ Water = 35 \times Weight(kg)$ | Hydration | ml/day |
| $BEE = 66 + 13.7W + 5H - 6.8A$ (men) | Basal Energy Expenditure (Harris-Benedict) | kcal |

## 2.4 Healthy Food — The Six Nutrient Groups

| Nutrient | Function | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrates | Energy (4 kcal/g) | Rice, wheat, fruits |
| Proteins | Building & repair (4 kcal/g) | Dal, eggs, fish, milk |
| Fats | Energy, insulation (9 kcal/g) | Nuts, oil, ghee |
| Vitamins | Bio-regulation | Fruits, vegetables |
| Minerals | Bone, nerve, fluid balance | Milk, leafy greens, salt |
| Water | Solvent, temperature regulation | Water, juices, fruits |

### Recommended Daily Intake (RDA — Approximate Adult)
- **Carbohydrates:** 45–65% of total calories
- **Proteins:** 0.8 g/kg body weight
- **Fats:** 20–35% of total calories
- **Fiber:** 25–30 g/day
- **Water:** 2.5–3.5 L/day
- **Salt:** < 5 g/day (WHO recommendation)

---

## 2.5 Depression, Anxiety, Stress — Biochemical Basis

### Neurotransmitters Involved
- **Serotonin** (mood, sleep, appetite) — low in depression
- **Dopamine** (pleasure, reward, motivation) — low in depression
- **Norepinephrine** (alertness, concentration) — altered in anxiety/depression
- **GABA** (calming) — low in anxiety
- **Cortisol** (stress hormone) — chronically elevated in chronic stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Physical exercise naturally boosts serotonin, dopamine, and endorphins — this is why exercise is prescribed as a first-line management for mild-to-moderate depression and anxiety.

### Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) — 6+ months of excessive worry
### Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) — 2+ weeks of persistent low mood

---

## 2.6 Carrying Techniques — Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **When to choose which carry (KTU 2024 decision tree):**

| Situation | Preferred Carry |
|---|---|
| Child, light adult, conscious | **Cradle carry** |
| Unconscious, far distance, rescuer alone | **Fireman's carry** |
| Two rescuers, conscious person | **Two-handed seat carry** |
| Spinal injury suspected | **DO NOT MOVE** (wait for ambulance with spinal board) |
| Person unable to walk, one rescuer | **Piggyback** |
| Emergency, building on fire | **Blanket drag** |
| Multiple injuries, multiple rescuers | **Improvised stretcher (3 or 4 rescuers)** |

---

## 2.7 AED — Technical Operation

### Types of AED
- **Semi-automatic:** User must press shock button after device advises
- **Fully automatic:** Delivers shock automatically after countdown

### AED Pad Placement (Adult)
- **Pad 1:** Right upper chest, below clavicle
- **Pad 2:** Left lower ribs, mid-axillary line (left side of chest)

### AED in Special Conditions
| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Wet patient | Dry chest before applying pads |
| Hairy chest | Shave quickly if pad won't stick |
| Pacemaker | Place pad > 8 cm away from device |
| Pregnant woman | AED is safe — same procedure |
| Infant (< 1 year) | Use manual defibrillator (AED not preferred) |
| Child (1–8 years) | Use pediatric pads if available, else use adult pads |

### Survival Statistics (Important for KTU Awareness)
- Each minute of delay in defibrillation reduces survival by **7–10%**.
- Survival rate drops from **70% at 1 min** to < 5% after 10 minutes without defibrillation.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYSIS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating BMI

**Problem:** A KTU engineering student weighs **72 kg** and is **1.70 m** tall. Calculate the BMI and interpret the result.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- Weight $W = 72 \text{ kg}$
- Height $H = 1.70 \text{ m}$

### Step 2: Apply the Formula
The standard BMI formula is:

$$BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values
First compute the square of height:

$$H^2 = (1.70)^2 = 2.89 \text{ m}^2$$

Now divide weight by height-squared:

$$BMI = \dfrac{72}{2.89}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Result
Performing the division:

$$BMI = 24.91 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Interpret Using WHO Classification
Looking at the KTU table:

- Range 18.5 – 24.9 = **Normal**
- Range 25.0 – 29.9 = **Overweight**

Since 24.91 falls just under 25, the student is in the **Normal weight** category, but is at the **upper borderline**.

### Step 6: Practical Recommendation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student should monitor diet, maintain regular physical activity (at least 150 min/week of moderate exercise), and re-evaluate BMI in 3–6 months to ensure it does not cross into the overweight category.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Target Heart Rate Calculation

**Problem:** A 25-year-old student wants to do aerobic exercise in the 70% intensity zone. Calculate the training heart rate.

### Step 1: Calculate Maximum Heart Rate
Using the standard formula:

$$HR_{max} = 220 - Age$$

Substitute age = 25:

$$HR_{max} = 220 - 25 = 195 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Target Heart Rate (70% Zone)
The training zone formula is:

$$HR_{target} = 0.70 \times HR_{max}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Compute
$$HR_{target} = 0.70 \times 195$$

$$HR_{target} = 136.5 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 4: Provide a Range for the Aerobic Zone (60–80%)
Lower bound (60%):

$$HR_{lower} = 0.60 \times 195 = 117 \text{ bpm}$$

Upper bound (80%):

$$HR_{upper} = 0.80 \times 195 = 156 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 5: Final Answer
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student should maintain their heart rate between **117 bpm and 156 bpm** during aerobic exercise. A practical target is **~137 bpm** at 70% intensity. This ensures cardiovascular benefit without overexertion.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Calorie Needs Estimation

**Problem:** Estimate the BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) of a 22-year-old male engineering student weighing 65 kg and 170 cm tall.

### Step 1: Apply Harris-Benedict Equation for Males
The formula is:

$$BMR = 66 + (13.7 \times W) + (5 \times H) - (6.8 \times A)$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight in kg
- $H$ = height in cm
- $A$ = age in years

### Step 2: Substitute the Values
Weight term:

$$13.7 \times 65 = 890.5$$

Height term:

$$5 \times 170 = 850$$

Age term:

$$6.8 \times 22 = 149.6$$

### Step 3: Sum All Terms
$$BMR = 66 + 890.5 + 850 - 149.6$$

Computing step by step:

$$BMR = 956.5 + 850 - 149.6$$

$$BMR = 1806.5 - 149.6$$

$$BMR = 1656.9 \text{ kcal/day}$$

### Step 4: Apply Activity Multiplier
For a sedentary student (multiplier = 1.2):

$$TDEE = BMR \times 1.2 = 1656.9 \times 1.2 = 1988.3 \text{ kcal/day}$$

For a moderately active student (multiplier = 1.55):

$$TDEE = 1656.9 \times 1.55 = 2568.2 \text{ kcal/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The student must consume approximately **2000–2600 kcal/day** depending on activity level. Falling below this leads to fatigue; exceeding it consistently leads to weight gain.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Decision Algorithm for AED Use

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal AED Algorithm (as per KTU 2024 / AHA Guidelines):**

**Step 1:** Confirm scene safety → safe to approach

**Step 2:** Check responsiveness → tap shoulder, shout "Are you OK?"

**Step 3:** Call emergency services (112 in India) → get AED

**Step 4:** Check breathing and pulse for not more than 10 seconds

**Step 5:** If no breathing, no pulse → Begin CPR (30 chest compressions : 2 rescue breaths)

**Step 6:** Turn ON AED as soon as available

**Step 7:** Attach pads (right clavicle, left axilla)

**Step 8:** Plug connector into AED (if not pre-connected)

**Step 9:** Stand clear → "Analyzing rhythm"

**Step 10:** If shock advised:
- Ensure no one is touching patient
- Loudly shout "STAND CLEAR!"
- Press shock button (semi-auto)

**Step 11:** Immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes

**Step 12:** AED will re-analyze → repeat shock if advised

**Step 13:** Continue until EMS arrives or patient regains pulse

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

### A. Mental Health Disorders Comparison

| Parameter | Stress | Anxiety | Depression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Acute or chronic | 6+ months (GAD) | 2+ weeks (MDD) |
| **Primary Emotion** | Overwhelm | Worry, fear | Sadness, emptiness |
| **Main Hormone** | Cortisol | Adrenaline | Cortisol + low serotonin |
| **Physical Symptoms** | Muscle tension | Palpitations, sweating | Fatigue, appetite change |
| **Treatment** | Lifestyle, relaxation | CBT, medication | CBT, medication (SSRI) |
| **Exercise Benefit** | High | High | High (evidence-based) |

### B. Carrying Techniques Comparison

| Carry | Rescuers | Conscious? | Spinal Safe? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cradle | 1 | Yes | No | Small children, light adult |
| Fireman's | 1 | No/Yes | No | Unconscious, strong rescuer |
| Piggyback | 1 | Yes | No | Conscious, can hold on |
| Two-handed seat | 2 | Yes | No | Conscious, can use arms |
| Four-handed seat | 2 | Yes | No | Conscious, both hands free |
| Drag/Sheet | 1 | No | No (with care) | Fire/smoke emergencies |
| Stretcher | 2+ | No/Yes | Yes (with cervical collar) | Serious injuries |

### C. AED vs CPR

| Parameter | CPR | AED |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Maintain circulation | Restore rhythm |
| **Action** | Manual compressions + breaths | Electrical shock |
| **Effectiveness** | Buys time | Definitive for VF/VT |
| **Combined use** | Always with AED if available | Always followed by CPR |
| **Survival boost** | Doubles/triples | 50–70% if within 3 min |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: Define Physical Education. List any four benefits of regular physical activity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Physical Education is a structured educational discipline that uses physical activity and movement to develop physical fitness, motor skills, cognitive understanding, and social behavior in individuals.

**Four Benefits:**
1. **Physiological:** Strengthens cardiovascular and respiratory systems, reduces risk of heart disease, diabetes, and obesity.
2. **Psychological:** Releases endorphins that elevate mood, reduces stress, anxiety, and symptoms of depression.
3. **Cognitive:** Improves concentration, memory, and academic performance.
4. **Social:** Develops teamwork, discipline, leadership, and sportsmanship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A clean definition (1 mark) + 4 benefits (0.5 mark each) is the standard. Avoid mixing up the categories.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q: What is an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)? When is it used?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An Automated External Defibrillator (AED) is a portable, lightweight, life-saving electronic device that automatically diagnoses life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias — specifically **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** and **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT)** — and delivers a controlled electric shock to restore the heart's normal rhythm.

**When Used:**
- In cases of **sudden cardiac arrest** where the patient is **unresponsive, not breathing, and has no pulse**.
- It is used as part of the **Chain of Survival** along with CPR.
- It is designed for use by **trained laypersons** and is commonly placed in airports, malls, gyms, and offices.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "AED restarts the heart" — this is partially correct. **Precise statement:** It *defibrillates* chaotic electrical activity; it does not start a stopped heart.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q: (a) Explain the Endocrine System in detail. List any five major endocrine glands with their hormones and functions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Endocrine System** is a collection of ductless glands that secrete chemical messengers called **hormones** directly into the bloodstream. These hormones travel to distant **target organs** to regulate metabolism, growth, reproduction, and behavior.

**Five Major Endocrine Glands:**

1. **Pituitary Gland (Master Gland)**
   - Location: Base of brain
   - Hormones: Growth Hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH
   - Function: Controls other endocrine glands, regulates growth

2. **Thyroid Gland**
   - Location: Front of neck
   - Hormones: Thyroxine (T4), Triiodothyronine (T3), Calcitonin
   - Function: Regulates metabolism, growth, calcium levels

3. **Adrenal Gland**
   - Location: Top of each kidney
   - Hormones: Adrenaline (Epinephrine), Cortisol, Aldosterone
   - Function: Fight-or-flight response, stress management, electrolyte balance

4. **Pancreas**
   - Location: Behind stomach
   - Hormones: Insulin (from beta cells), Glucagon (from alpha cells)
   - Function: Regulates blood glucose (insulin lowers, glucagon raises)

5. **Gonads (Ovaries in females, Testes in males)**
   - Location: Pelvic cavity / scrotum
   - Hormones: Estrogen, Progesterone (females); Testosterone (males)
   - Function: Development of secondary sexual characteristics, reproduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the **hormone name AND its function** is mandatory. A student who lists only glands without hormones loses 1 mark.

---

**Q: (b) What is Depression? Discuss its symptoms, causes, and management strategies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Depression (Major Depressive Disorder) is a common, serious mood disorder characterized by persistent feelings of sadness, loss of interest (anhedonia), and impaired daily functioning lasting **at least 2 weeks**.

**Symptoms:**
1. **Emotional:** Persistent low mood, hopelessness, irritability, feelings of guilt
2. **Cognitive:** Difficulty concentrating, indecisiveness, negative thoughts
3. **Physical:** Fatigue, sleep disturbance (insomnia or hypersomnia), appetite changes
4. **Behavioral:** Social withdrawal, reduced activity, loss of interest in hobbies
5. **Severe:** Suicidal thoughts or attempts (requires emergency care)

**Causes:**
- **Biological:** Low levels of serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine; hormonal imbalance
- **Psychological:** Childhood trauma, low self-esteem, chronic stress
- **Social:** Loss of loved one, relationship problems, academic/financial stress
- **Medical:** Chronic illness, certain medications, substance abuse

**Management Strategies:**
1. **Psychotherapy:** Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Interpersonal Therapy
2. **Medication:** Antidepressants (SSRIs like fluoxetine, SNRIs)
3. **Lifestyle:** Regular exercise (proven to boost serotonin), adequate sleep, balanced diet
4. **Social Support:** Family, friends, support groups
5. **Mindfulness:** Meditation, yoga, deep breathing
6. **Professional Help:** Psychiatrist consultation in moderate-severe cases

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do NOT write "depression is just sadness" or "one can snap out of it." Depression is a **clinical disorder** requiring medical attention. Writing this in the exam will cost 1–2 marks.

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q: (a) Define Fitness Profile. Explain the components of health-related fitness with their tests. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **Fitness Profile** is a comprehensive evaluation of an individual's health and physical status, measured across multiple parameters to identify strengths, weaknesses, and risk factors for lifestyle diseases.

**Components of Health-Related Fitness:**

1. **Cardiovascular Endurance**
   - Definition: Ability of heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply oxygen during sustained activity
   - Test: **Cooper 12-minute run test**, **1.5-mile run test**
   - Improvement: Running, cycling, swimming, aerobics

2. **Muscular Strength**
   - Definition: Maximum force a muscle can exert in a single effort
   - Test: **Handgrip dynamometer test**, **1-Repetition Max (1RM) test**
   - Improvement: Weight training, resistance bands

3. **Muscular Endurance**
   - Definition: Ability of muscles to sustain repeated contractions over time
   - Test: **Push-up test**, **Sit-up test**, **Plank test**
   - Improvement: Calisthenics, circuit training

4. **Flexibility**
   - Definition: Range of motion around a joint
   - Test: **Sit-and-reach test**, **Shoulder stretch test**
   - Improvement: Stretching, yoga, Pilates

5. **Body Composition**
   - Definition: Ratio of fat mass to lean mass in the body
   - Test: **BMI calculation**, **Skinfold caliper test**, **Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA)**
   - Goal: Maintain healthy fat percentage (men: 10–20%, women: 18–28%)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair each component with a **test name** and a **method of improvement** for full marks.

---

**Q: (b) What are processed and ultra-processed foods? Explain their health hazards and list five healthy eating habits. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Processed Foods:** Foods that have been altered from their natural state through addition of salt, sugar, oil, or preservatives to enhance taste, shelf-life, or convenience. Example: Canned vegetables, cheese, bread.

**Ultra-Processed Foods (UPF):** Industrial formulations made mostly from substances derived from foods plus cosmetic additives. Example: Soft drinks, chips, instant noodles, packaged snacks, ready-to-eat meals.

**Health Hazards of Ultra-Processed Foods:**
1. **Obesity:** High in calories, low in nutrients, leads to overeating
2. **Type 2 Diabetes:** Excess sugar → insulin resistance
3. **Cardiovascular Disease:** High salt → hypertension; trans fats → atherosclerosis
4. **Fatty Liver (NAFLD):** High fructose and refined carbs
5. **Mental Health Issues:** Linked to depression and anxiety in studies
6. **Cancer Risk:** WHO classifies processed meats as Group 1 carcinogen
7. **Dental Caries:** Sugar promotes bacterial growth in mouth

**Five Healthy Eating Habits:**
1. **Eat a balanced diet** following the plate model (½ vegetables, ¼ protein, ¼ grains)
2. **Have regular meal timings** — never skip breakfast
3. **Drink adequate water** — minimum 2.5 to 3 L per day; avoid sugary drinks
4. **Limit sugar, salt, and oil** — WHO recommends < 5 g salt/day, < 25 g added sugar/day
5. **Practice mindful eating** — eat without screens, chew slowly, stop when 80% full
6. **Include fruits and vegetables daily** — minimum 5 servings per day
7. **Cook at home** — reduces dependence on processed food

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "processed" with "ultra-processed" and lose marks. Be precise: *Cheese is processed; chips and soft drinks are **ultra-processed***.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Final Sprint Before KTU Exam)**

### Physical Education
- ☐ Definition includes *educational* and *developmental* aspects
- ☐ 4 Pillars: Health-related, Skill-related, Mental, Social Fitness
- ☐ WHO recommendation: **150 min/week** moderate or **75 min/week** vigorous activity
- ☐ Interdisciplinary links: relevant to ALL branches of engineering

### Endocrine System
- ☐ Master Gland = **Pituitary**; Master Controller = **Hypothalamus**
- ☐ Hormones vs. Nerves: chemical, slow, long-lasting vs. electrical, fast, short
- ☐ Insulin (pancreas) lowers blood sugar; Glucagon raises it
- ☐ Cortisol = stress hormone (adrenal cortex); Adrenaline = fight-or-flight (adrenal medulla)
- ☐ Negative feedback maintains homeostasis

### Fitness Profile
- ☐ BMI formula: $W \div H^2$; normal range 18.5–24.9
- ☐ $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$; Training zone 60–80%
- ☐ Components + Tests: CV Endurance (Cooper), Strength (Dynamometer), Endurance (Push-ups), Flexibility (Sit-and-reach), Composition (BMI/Skinfold)

### Healthy Food & Lifestyle Diseases
- ☐ Six nutrients: Carbs, Proteins, Fats, Vitamins, Minerals, Water
- ☐ Plate Model: ½ veg, ¼ grain, ¼ protein
- ☐ RDA: Protein 0.8 g/kg body weight; Salt < 5 g/day; Sugar < 25 g/day

### Processed Foods
- ☐ NOVA classification: 1 (Unprocessed) → 4 (Ultra-processed)
- ☐ Examples of UPF: soft drinks, chips, instant noodles, packaged snacks
- ☐ Diseases: Obesity, T2DM, CVD, NAFLD, Cancer, Depression

### Depression, Anxiety, Stress
- ☐ Cortisol + low serotonin + low dopamine = depression
- ☐ GAD requires 6+ months; MDD requires 2+ weeks
- ☐ First-line non-drug treatment: exercise + CBT + sleep hygiene

### Carrying Injured
- ☐ **Golden Rule:** Do NOT move unless danger
- ☐ Spinal injury → use spinal board, do not lift manually
- ☐ One rescuer: Cradle (light) / Fireman (unconscious)
- ☐ Two rescuers: Two-handed seat / Four-handed seat

### AED
- ☐ Used in **sudden cardiac arrest** with no pulse, no breathing
- ☐ Pad placement: **Right clavicle** + **Left axilla**
- ☐ Each minute delay = 7–10% lower survival
- ☐ Always resume CPR after shock
- ☐ Do not use in water; keep oxygen 1 m away; pediatric pads for child < 8 years
- ☐ **Shout "STAND CLEAR"** before pressing shock button
